data.title: Sadiq Khan could wage war on ‘Chelsea tractor’ owners with new charges
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is considering waging a war on “Chelsea tractor” owners with new charges, a new document suggests.

Transport for London’s (TfL) Vision Zero action plan claimed there was a “pressing need for targeted safety interventions” against larger vehicles.

It stated the size of large SUVs – nicknamed “Chelsea tractors” – made roads “more hazardous for everybody outside the vehicle” because they “significantly increase the severity of collisions”.

The plan is part of Sir Sadiq’s efforts to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on London’s roads by 2041.

There is no specific definition of an SUV, but it is a term given to vehicles that are generally taller, wider, heavier and less fuel-efficient than traditional cars. Critics have pointed out that electric vehicles (EVs) are just as heavy, but would not be affected by the plans.

Thomas Turrell, a member of the London Assembly, said: “This family car tax is a double standard. The mayor will not go after his precious heavy EV cars with this regressive, backwards tax, instead choosing to hammer families.

“His anti-car zealotry will not stop here. If he truly believes vehicles should be taxed for being heavier, he should be consistent rather than money-grabbing.”

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said all cars sold in the UK “regardless of size” must meet “exacting safety and pedestrian protection standards”.

He added: “Singling out specific cars based on size restricts consumer choice and would unfairly penalise the many drivers who require a larger vehicle for essential mobility.”

No proposals about possible charges for SUVs have been put forward yet.

SUVs account for about a third of all new cars sold in the UK. They have grown in popularity in recent years, with many drivers favouring their higher seating position.

The Vision Zero document claimed “oversized” vehicles such as SUVs made it “harder for drivers to see people walking and cycling nearby”, adding that children were “more easily hidden from a driver’s view”.

It stated that in Britain, SUVs were 14 per cent more likely to kill pedestrians and cyclists than other passenger cars if there was a collision, and 77 per cent more likely to kill children.

Punishing London’s motorists

Sir Sadiq expanded the ultra-low-emission zone to cover the whole of London in August 2023, which requires drivers of vehicles that do not meet emissions standards to pay a daily fee of £12.50.

London also has an £18 congestion charge for driving in a zone in the centre of the capital at certain times.

Sir Sadiq has faced sustained criticism from ordinary Londoners that he ignores their concerns about schemes that punish motorists.

One such scheme was formally approved this week, which involves the construction of a cycle path at the Holland Park roundabout in west London, one of the capital’s busiest road interchanges.

Residents have repeatedly objected to the £6m scheme and have claimed that TfL has cited false statistics generated to justify the works.

Ruth Saunders, a member of the Save Our Streets group based near the roundabout, said: “This scheme is designed to benefit a small number of commuter cyclists travelling through the borough for a few hours each day, to the detriment of local residents and other road users.”

Regarding the potential plans to tax SUVs, a spokesman for Sir Sadiq claimed: “There is growing evidence about the safety risks of large SUVs, which are becoming more common on London’s roads.

“The mayor has asked TfL to undertake detailed analysis of the safety risks posed by large SUVs and their wider impact on London’s roads.

“This could then inform any future policy proposals in London or recommendations to Government or the sector.

“As the numbers of large SUVs increase on London’s roads, it is right that TfL consider their impact on safety and how they affect other road users.”

data.title: M&S accuses Labour of driving up energy bills
data.description:
M&S has accused the Government of driving up energy bills as Britain faces one of the biggest hits to growth from the war in Iran.

Stuart Machin, who runs the retail giant, said government-imposed levies now accounted for more than half of his company’s energy costs and had “nothing to do with the price of oil or gas”.

“Over the last few years the ‘policy costs’ on our energy bill have skyrocketed,” Mr Machin wrote on LinkedIn.

“These are the tariffs that [the Government] place on our bills to fund their policies, and have nothing to do with the price of oil or gas. They now make up over half our bill. It’s just not sustainable for UK businesses.”

The comments came as the OECD said Britain faced the biggest hit from surging oil and gas prices out of any major economy.

Inflation will surge more sharply than in other developed nations and wipe half a percentage point off UK growth this year, according to its forecast.

The UK is particularly exposed because of its heavy reliance on imported energy. Oil prices have risen from $70 per barrel a month ago to around $100 today, while natural gas prices have nearly doubled since the start of the war in Iran.

The OECD’s forecast is a blow to the Government, which before the start of the conflict in the Gulf had hoped that cost-of-living pressures would ease this year.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, said the UK had not started the war but that it would have “an impact on our country”. She insisted Labour had “the right economic plan”.

Mr Machin’s frustration with government policy stems from green energy levies added to the wholesale price of energy.

They include a so-called renewables obligation, under which suppliers must source a proportion of power from renewable generators or pay a penalty; a scheme that funds clean energy projects; “feed-in tariffs” for small-scale generators and charges that help ensure enough power is on standby.

Beyond policy costs, businesses must also pay network charges, which fund upgrades to Britain’s power network, much of it required to meet net zero, and the climate change levy, a tax on non-domestic energy consumption.

A report by the Confederation of British Industry and Energy UK found British businesses were paying 60pc more for gas and 70pc more for electricity than they did before the energy crisis caused by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022. That is despite wholesale prices falling “substantially” in the same period.

Separately, the M&S boss wrote on Thursday to Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood to raise concerns about a surge in “significant” crime in his stores.

Speaking at the Business Leader Summit in London, Mr Machin said: “All I keep hearing about is crime going down and that’s not what we’re seeing in the real world. Crime is going up.”

Mr Machin said he had also written to the Chancellor earlier this week setting out “very clear views” about issues facing British businesses.

Blow to growth

The OECD predicted that the UK economy will grow by 0.7pc this year, far below the 1.2pc it forecast for the UK just three months ago. No other country in the G20 suffered a larger downgrade. France and Germany are each expected to grow by 0.8pc, a downgrade from December’s projection of 1pc.

Only Russia and Italy are on track for weaker growth this year.

At the same time, inflation is expected to jump back to 4pc. That is double the Bank of England’s 2pc target and well above the 2.5pc anticipated before the war.

It means families and businesses face another year of mediocre economic growth combined with painful rises in living costs. High inflation also raises the prospect that the Bank of England will keep interest rates higher for longer.

Asa Johansson, a director at the OECD economics department, said “relatively weak” growth at the end of last year had left the UK exposed.

“That meant that the momentum going into this year was weaker, which meant less positive upside factors offsetting the negative energy price shock,” she said.

The Paris-based institution said all countries that rely on energy imports for their power would suffer a shock from higher oil and gas prices.

It also warned that “those with relatively low energy inventory levels” are especially vulnerable. Britain only has enough gas stored to cover a few days of consumption, compared with several weeks’ worth in other major European economies.

UK ‘exposed’

Sir Mel Stride, the shadow chancellor, said the Government had left the UK exposed to the crisis.

“Rachel Reeves has ramped up borrowing, spending and taxes. As a result we have stagnant growth, while inflation, unemployment, the deficit and debt interest costs have all shot up,” he said.

“At the same time, Ed Miliband’s net zero obsession has left us reliant on imported energy instead of using our own supplies in the North Sea.”

The OECD warned that energy prices could remain high long after the conflict ended and said the crisis was likely to push up food prices too.

Financial markets expect the Bank to raise interest rates by between two and four times in the next year to crack down on inflation. It represents a stark turnaround from expectations just a few weeks ago of two rate cuts this year.

The OECD predicts the Bank will act more cautiously, holding rates at 3.75pc this year and cutting borrowing costs to 3.5pc in early 2027, supporting growth next year.

Sarah Breeden, a deputy Governor of the Bank of England, said there was not “a straight line between oil and gas prices” and interest rates.

However, she said it was “clear there’s going to be an impact on [housing] affordability because mortgage rates have risen” in response to expectations of possible interest rate increases.

Ms Breeden said higher mortgage rates were unlikely to crash the housing market. She said: “Our analysis suggests that even with higher energy prices, even with higher interest rates, there should be enough resilience in borrowers to stop the bust. We haven’t had the boom, we shouldn’t have the bust.”

Alan Taylor, an external member of the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee, warned that “the only thing that is sure is that there are tough times ahead”.

In a speech in New York, he said: “It is likely that energy prices will be significantly higher from here on for a significant period of time.”

Ms Reeves said the impact of the war was unavoidable.

“The war in the Middle East is not one that we started, nor is it a war that we have joined. But it is a war that will have an impact on our country,” she said.

“In an uncertain world we have the right economic plan. The decisions we have taken have put us in a better position to protect the country’s finances and family finances from global instability.

“Our economic plan means going further to build a stronger, more secure economy. That means going further on our three big choices: empowering regional growth, embracing AI and innovation and establishing a closer relationship with the EU.”

Pressure is mounting on the Chancellor to launch a package of support for households and businesses with soaring energy bills.

However, the OECD cautioned against launching the kind of sweeping subsidies Britain introduced following 2022’s energy price spike.

“Any new discretionary measures should be well-targeted on households most in need and viable firms, preserve incentives to lower energy use, and have clear expiry mechanisms,” the OECD said.

Ms Reeves has promised targeted support for low-income families.

While Britain will suffer, the OECD said some other nations were benefitting from “continued strong momentum” in AI.

The US economy is on track to grow by 2pc this year, up from earlier forecasts of 1.7pc.

A government spokesman said: “We know that businesses across Britain will be concerned about the impact on their energy bills from events in the Middle East.

“Wholesale costs are the single largest component of energy prices.

“Our mission for clean power by 2030 will get us off the roller-coaster of fossil fuel prices, to cut bills for businesses and households for good.”

data.title: Married At First Sight star dies after cancer battle
data.description:
Married At First Sight’s Mel Schilling has died at the age of 54, days after revealing her colon cancer had spread to her brain.

Schilling, who was a relationship coach on the Channel 4 reality television series, was diagnosed with cancer in December 2023.

But in an Instagram post last week, the TV star said the disease had spread first to her lungs and then her brain.

In a statement published on Tuesday, her husband Gareth Brisbane said: “To most of you, she was Mel Schilling – matriarch of MAFS and queen of reality TV. To Maddie and me, she was our wee Melsie: an incredible mum, role model, and soulmate.

“On behalf of our family and her incredible friendship group, thank you for the support from around the world. Life can be beautiful, and life can be incredibly cruel. But ultimately, life is fleeting, fragile, and tomorrow is promised to no one.

“If you can do anything to honour Mel, please live life to the full, love your people well, and try not to sweat the small stuff. I had 15 wonderful years with my soulmate, and it was the privilege of my life to be by her side. For that, I will be forever thankful.

“Goodbye, my love. My one. Until we meet again.”

CPL Productions, which makes Married At First Sight UK, said it was “deeply saddened” by her death.

“Over many years, many of us have been privileged to work with Mel on Married At First Sight, and her loss today will come as a tremendous sadness to all who knew her,” the company said in a statement.

“Mel went far beyond being just a fantastic on-screen presenter; she was a friend to CPL and to many of us who worked with her.

“We send our love and heartfelt condolences at this incredibly difficult time to Mel’s family, friends, and all who were close to her. She will be deeply missed by everyone whose lives she touched.”

Channel 4 described her as “someone who radiated joy, warmth and optimism, who energised every room she walked into, with humour and positivity”.

In a statement, the station said: “All of us at Channel 4 are incredibly saddened by the news of Mel’s death. Our thoughts and condolences are, first and foremost, with her family and loved ones.

“We’re privileged to be the channel that is home to Mel’s work, which was at the heart of Married At First Sight’s phenomenal success, both in the UK and Australia. It reflected so much about her – her fierce advocacy for other women, her passion for healthy relationships and her mission to unite people in love.

“Just as importantly, we’d like to pay tribute to Mel as a person. For many who work for Channel 4, Mel was not just a colleague but a friend, someone who radiated joy, warmth and optimism, who energised every room she walked into, with humour and positivity.”

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”

data.title: Royal Mail deserves appreciation for delivering post, says billionaire owner
data.description:
The billionaire owner of Royal Mail has told MPs that it deserves appreciation for delivering the post after it was described as “a national institution in meltdown”.

Czech tycoon Daniel Křetínský said Royal Mail was doing “a hard job that no one else in Europe is doing” and “the company and the people [Royal Mail employees] deserve appreciation for that”.

He pointed to postal services in other European countries where first-class post is three times more expensive than in the UK.

“If you decide to send a [first-class] letter from Brighton to the Scottish Highlands, you have to get it delivered the next day for £1.80,” he said. “It is not an easy job.”

Mr Křetínský’s defiant stance came as he was questioned by MPs on the Business and Select Trade Committee over mounting concerns about declining service levels.

Before Tuesday’s hearing, Liam Byrne, a Labour MP and the committee chairman, had called the Royal Mail “a national institution in meltdown”.

However, Mr Křetínský denied accusations that performance had deteriorated since his investment group EP bought the Royal Mail’s parent company, International Distribution Services, last year for £3.6bn.

The hearing was triggered by an investigation that found just 74.9pc of first-class post was delivered on time between April 2025 and January 2026 – way below a target of 93pc.

MPs found the discrepancy was equivalent to around 126 million first-class letters arriving late over the year.

With nearly 10pc of second-class letters also falling short of targets, it means a total of 219 million letters will arrive late this year, the committee has calculated.

Mr Křetínský acknowledged that Royal Mail was “not delivering on its promises” but refuted “the suggestion that service is declining”.

The billionaire businessman, who had opened his testimony by stating he was “a numbers person”, said he was “sorry for any letter that arrives late” but claimed “the numbers” don’t support claims of a drop in performance.

“Our quality of service, unfortunately, is relatively consistent over the last three years,” he added.

Asked by Mr Byrne whether he wanted “the committee to believe that service is currently adequate”, Mr Křetínský replied: “No, but there has been no decline in service. They are two different things.”

He also rebutted widespread claims that Royal Mail was routinely prioritising more lucrative parcel deliveries over letters, which are in decline.

In the previous session, trade union bosses had told the committee that workers were being instructed to leave NHS letters on racks and prioritise parcels instead.

“All employees want to deliver and some of them feel very aggrieved that they’re told to leave doctors’ and hospital letters in the frames to prioritise tracked [post],” Martin Walsh, deputy general secretary of the Communication Workers Union (CWU), said.

Mr Walsh said Royal Mail’s problems were being exacerbated by a “retention crisis” in which postal staff are “working harder than they’ve ever done in really challenging conditions, because they can’t clear the workload every day”.

Royal Mail bosses were also questioned about concerns that May’s local elections could be jeopardised by the late delivery of postal votes and election leaflets.

“Yes or no, are you guaranteeing to deliver postal votes to the country so that it can vote in elections on time?” Mr Byrne asked.

“We have put all planning in place so we are ready to deliver democracy for this country,” Alistair Cochrane, Royal Mail’s chief executive, said.

Mr Cochrane told the committee that Royal Mail is on course to deliver between 5.6 billion and 5.7 billion letters this year, compared with 6.3 billion last year and 20 billion in 2024.

data.title: ‘My father-in-law died, then NS&I locked us out of his £50k savings’
data.description:
Have you been affected by NS&I delaying or withholding payouts? Email us at money@telegraph.co.uk*.

Bereaved families are considering court action after years of “stressful” dealings with National Savings and Investments (NS&I), which they say lost track of their loved ones’ savings after death.

Customers have described a total breakdown in communication with the provider and said they would pull their own money from accounts to avoid the same ordeal.

It comes as an investigation by The Telegraph found that taxpayers could be on the hook for a bill of up to £400m to return missing funds to families.

Retired nurse Wendy Maxted, from Ramsgate, Kent, spent months chasing NS&I for updates on her late father-in-law’s £50,000 premium bonds savings.

Ms Maxted, 64, who has been processing the loss of her mother and the pressure of having to retire early because of ill health, said the stress became unbearable.

When her father-in-law died last March, Ms Maxted took the lead on the paperwork and administration. But she didn’t expect that it would take a year in total to withdraw the funds.

She said: “It was stressful enough, but this just added so much more stress. I tried to phone, I did an online form – I never got any response.”

Eventually, Ms Maxted’s mother-in-law received a letter informing her that because of the amount held, a grant of probate would be required to release the funds.

Five months passed between the family getting probate and the money finally being released by NS&I, according to Ms Maxted.

The solicitor dealing with the matter visited Wendy’s mother-in-law at home as she was so frail.

“On top of losing her husband, she’s faced a battle to get his money out,” Ms Maxted said.

“My mother-in-law is withdrawing her own £50,000 in [premium bonds] because of the experience... She doesn’t want to put her sons through the same situation.”

Ms Maxted said it cost her family £1,800 in legal fees to access her father-in-law’s savings.

“It just seems so wrong,” she said.

Dozens of bereaved family members have contacted The Telegraph describing similar struggles. The government-backed bank has been accused of “short-changing” them as they have struggled to access cash that is rightfully theirs.

NS&I holds some £240bn for more than 24 million savers, making it one of Britain’s biggest banks.

But it has long said that a grant of probate or a letter of administration may be needed for anyone deceased with savings or premium bonds in excess of £5,000.

‘It’s been a horror story’

Graham Street, from Preston, described his own dealings with NS&I as a “horror story”. In a tearful conversation, he said he had faced sleepless nights with letters from NS&I and lawyers spread out around him.

His parents lived in Spain for most of their adult lives. In June 2017, his mother purchased £10,000 of premium bonds with NS&I.

When she passed away in 2021, an administrative nightmare began. Passed between NS&I and Spanish notaries, Mr Street said he had received conflicting advice on how to handle the probate process.

To add insult to injury, Mr Street said NS&I sent two letters to his mother with news of £100 and £50 wins. These came after the firm was informed she had died.

“It was horrifying,” Mr Street said.

Years later, he still hasn’t been able to access the funds.

He said that communication had gone “downhill” in the past few years, with referrals to a “backroom team” that he was not able to contact, and promises of letters that did not arrive.

Mr Street promised his father in the month before his passing last January that he would “get the money back”.

“It was extremely upsetting,” he said. “I was making a promise I wasn’t sure I would be able to carry out.”

He is now considering court action.

The 160-year-old institution has already faced criticism for spending billions of pounds of taxpayers’ money on a stalled digital transformation.

The public accounts committee called it a “full-spectrum disaster” in a report last month.

‘NS&I sent a letter to someone who died 16 years ago’

While financial planner Phil Billingham does recommend NS&I products to his clients at Perceptive Planning, he questions whether the firm understands the difficulties bereaved families face.

Before his own mother and father went into care, they cleared out a lot of paperwork.

His father, who had invested about £30,000 in premium bonds, died in 2021. After his death, Mr Billingham had to trawl through his belongings to find a letter from NS&I with his father’s bondholder number on it to trace the money.

“I knew it was there, and I work in financial services. I knew how to find the money, but a lay-person wouldn’t have been able to do it.”

Mr Billingham said he also received two letters to his home with NS&I prizes addressed to deceased people.

“One of them has been dead for about 16 years, but still there is post turning up with winnings,” he said.

Mr Billingham suggested that NS&I’s “refusal” to deal with email slows processes down, creating further complications for grieving clients.

“Everything has to be posted, they think that is looking after taxpayers’ money,” he added, referring to the fact that the savings body is backed by the Treasury.

Simon Kent told The Telegraph that he informed NS&I of his mother’s death 25 days ago, with little response. She held about £1,400 in premium bonds.

“It’s not a large amount, it’s just the frustration more than anything,” he said.

Other readers told us about a lack of communication. One said he had been trying to use the NS&I tracing service to track down premium bonds for more than a year.

Another, called Chris, said that as executor for his mother, he was awarded probate in January. He said everything related to his mother’s estate has been tied up – apart from NS&I, where he is struggling to get it to close the account which holds £100. He said this was despite calling repeatedly and filing the necessary paperwork.

An NS&I spokesman said it recognised that dealing with bereavement could be “challenging”.

The firm also offered an apology “to anyone who has not received the customer service from NS&I that they should expect, particularly at such a sensitive time”.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Lloyds to pay compensation after mass data leak
data.description:
Lloyds has been forced to pay compensation after a data breach affected half a million customers.

More than 400,000 customers at Lloyds and its sister banks Halifax and the Bank of Scotland were able to see each other’s transactions in an IT glitch earlier this month.

The error has forced Lloyds to pay a total of £139,000 in compensation to 3,625 customers for “distress and inconvenience” as of Monday.

None of them has yet been identified as having suffered a financial loss because of the technical failure.

The breach, which occurred on March 12, led to sensitive data being shared between customers at the banks and affected 447,936 people.

Under the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) consumer duty rules, banks must put customers’ needs first and treat customers fairly in cases of financial distress.

Their account information, National Insurance numbers and payment references were shared between users on mobile banking apps.

In a letter to the Treasury select committee of MPs today, Lloyds revealed that people who were not its customers may also have been visible.

It said this was because, in some cases, information about people who use other banks could have been visible on payments they made to Lloyds customers.

Last March, the committee found nine of the top banks had accumulated at least 33 days’ worth of IT outages over the preceding two years.

Dame Meg Hillier, the Treasury committee chairman, said: “Modern banking methods mean we can now perform a variety of tasks on our phones in a matter of seconds, and almost anywhere.

“What this incident brings into focus is the fact that there is a trade-off. By moving more interactions with our bank online, we place our faith in technology, which can suffer unpredictable errors.”

Last March, the committee found nine of the top banks had accumulated at least 33 days’ worth of IT outages over the preceding two years.

There were at least 158 IT failures between January 2023 and February 2025, lasting the equivalent of 33 days, it found.

Dame Meg added: “It’s critical that consumers understand this, and that’s why my committee continues to push banks to be transparent when things go wrong.”

The Lloyds outage occurred overnight after the lender updated its software, inadvertently creating a vulnerability in its customer-facing system. The problem was resolved within hours.

The bank is urging customers to delete any screenshots of accounts they recorded and shared online that were not theirs because of the error. It added that there was “no evidence” of fraud having arisen from the incident.

A Lloyds Banking Group spokesman said: “On March 12, some customers using our app may have briefly seen transactions that were not their own following an IT change. The issue was quickly identified and resolved, and we’ve contacted customers whose transactions may have been visible for that short time.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: 120-year-old British wheel factory at risk of closure
data.description:
A 120-year-old British wheel factory is at risk of closure after its German owners called in the administrators.

Moveero, which makes wheels, rims and hubs for off-road vehicles used in farming and construction, said its UK arm had become insolvent on Thursday, putting around 250 jobs at risk.

Administrators from Interpath have been appointed to run the struggling business, which trades from a historic factory in Telford, Shropshire.

Its customers include JCB and Caterpillar, among others, with its products used for the wheels of everything from earthmovers to airport tugs and tree harvesters.

The business, dating back to 1908, was part of GKN’s automotive division but was sold to German private equity firm Aurelius in 2020.

Aurelius was also behind a disastrous buyout of ethical beauty chain the Body Shop, which collapsed just five months after its new owners took control.

In a statement on Thursday, Ryan Grant, Chris Pole and Sam Birchall, administrators at Interpath, said Moveero had been battling “significant operational challenges”, including weak demand for its products and tough competition.

Rising interest rates have made buying heavy equipment more difficult for farmers and construction companies, along with trade tariffs imposed on imports by the likes of the US and China.

Moveero Ltd reported a loss of £4.4m in 2024, the latest year for which there are accounts, after sales fell 26pc to £32.6m.

The wider Moveero group, which includes businesses in the US and Denmark, continues to operate profitably and is not insolvent.

In the UK, administrators have been appointed to “protect the interests of creditors”.

A statement said this would “allow the UK businesses to continue trading while buyers for the businesses and their assets are pursued, with all staff retained during this period”.

David Geraghty, Moveero’s chief executive, said the company’s customers had also provided emergency funding that had “given us the additional time we need to continue to explore the options available”.

Mr Grant, the managing director at Interpath, added: “We have been pleased with the positive conversations and financial support provided by customers in recent days, which has ultimately provided the businesses with a crucial lifeline, enabling us to trade while we explore options for the business.”

Interpath said its “immediate priority is to seek buyers for the UK businesses and assets” and urged potential buyers to come forward as soon as possible.

Aurelius has been approached for comment.

data.title: Labour risks ‘banning’ Saturday jobs, warns M&S
data.description:
The boss of Marks & Spencer has warned Labour it risked destroying Saturday jobs by overhauling workers’ rights.

Stuart Machin suggested that the introduction of new rules to guarantee weekly staff hours risked backfiring by preventing employers from hiring part-time weekend workers.

This would prove particularly problematic for those trying to take their “first step into work”, Mr Machin said.

Writing in a blog post for Alan Milburn, Sir Keir Starmer’s employment tsar, Mr Machin said: “New regulations dissuade employers from offering a shift or two a week, and the red tape around work experience often makes it prohibitive to offer.

“We’re at risk of not only disincentivising the Saturday job, but banning it.”

The warning comes as companies brace for a new wave of red tape under Labour’s sweeping workers’ rights shake-up.

Although the Employment Rights Bill received royal assent late last year, various issues are still under negotiation, and are expected to be finalised in secondary legislation.

This includes rules around guaranteed work for those on “low” and “zero-hours” contracts.

Union bosses have been urging Sir Keir to hand all workers the right to guaranteed hours.

However, retailers have raised concerns that this will make it harder to give part-time workers extra shifts.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise, the boss of Next, which employs 20,000 part-time staff, has previously said this change would leave shops “chronically overstaffed”.

He said it would force retailers to employ temporary staff to cover seasonal demand, such as the busy Christmas period, rather than offer more shifts to part-time workers who typically pick up work on Saturdays or Sundays.

This would “deprive loyal and skilled employees of income at times when they need it”, Lord Wolfson said.

Speaking on Thursday, the Next boss also raised concerns about Labour’s plans to speed up minimum wage increases for younger staff.

In its manifesto before the election, Labour pledged to pay young people the same minimum wage as older workers.

Lord Wolfson said: “It doesn’t feel like the right time. With everything that we’ve been saying about entry-level jobs being difficult, it doesn’t feel like the right time – even if it’s the right thing to do long term.”

Youth unemployment recently jumped to an 11-year high, with at least 16.1pc of 16 to 24-year-olds seeking work not yet in a job, according to the Office for National Statistics.

Increases in the minimum wage have led to almost a third of businesses reducing entry-level roles, with half looking to hire more older workers instead, according to an study from accounting firm RSM on Friday.

Mr Machin called for the Government to focus on the importance of retail to boost employment.

He said: “Retail and hospitality have been left out of most visions for economic growth. Politicians momentarily become interested in the ‘everyday economy’, but they quickly become distracted by flashier industries.

“That lack of interest is reflected in the range of cost pressures facing retail: business rates, packaging taxes, energy tariffs, national insurance rises. If we valued retail as the first rung on the jobs ladder we would surely take a different approach.”

It marks Mr Machin’s second political intervention in as many days as the retail boss also accused the Government on Thursday of driving up energy bills through a series of green levies.

Posting on LinkedIn, he said: “Over the last few years the ‘policy costs’ on our energy bill have skyrocketed.

“These are the tariffs that [the Government] place on our bills to fund their policies, and have nothing to do with the price of oil or gas. They now make up over half our bill. It’s just not sustainable for UK businesses.”

data.title: Europe facing fuel shortage within days, warns Shell boss
data.description:
Europe risks fuel shortages as soon as next month as the fallout from the Middle East crisis deepens, the boss of Shell has warned.

Wael Sawan, the company’s chief executive, said the global oil and gas supply squeeze had already forced parts of Asia to cut energy consumption and that the “ripple effect” threatens to spread west within days.

He warned this meant European governments may need to curb energy demand – a step not taken since the 2022 energy crisis – to prevent shortages.

Around one fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG) supplies are trapped in the Gulf following Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Military sources said on Tuesday that Britain was leading efforts to reopen the vital trade choke point, with planners considering deploying mine-hunting drones from chartered civilian “motherships”.

Meanwhile, Donald Trump is preparing to send in paratroopers for a possible ground invasion of key islands along the Iranian coast. Yet, the US president also insisted on Tuesday that peace talks with Tehran were taking place.

JD Vance, the vice-president, is being lined up as a negotiator for the first time as Iran is refusing to talk with Mr Trump’s Middle East envoys, sources told The Telegraph.

Gulf sources said that the Iranians would not meet Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, accusing them of “backstabbing” at previous nuclear talks before the war started.

With the conflict now in its fourth week, the International Energy Agency has called on countries to reduce oil and gas consumption by encouraging more remote working, lower road speed limits, and greater use of public transport.

In Asia, where countries are heavily reliant on oil and gas from the Middle East, governments have already imposed four-day working weeks, urged people to use less air conditioning and suspended overseas trips for bureaucrats.

Speaking at an industry conference in Houston, Texas, Mr Sawan said European governments may soon need to take similar measures.

“It is a ripple effect,” he said. “We see south Asia first to get that brunt, that moves to south-east Asia, north-east Asia and then more so into Europe as we get into April.

“So we are trying to work with governments to alert them to the levers they may need to pull – including demand‑side measures, what they need to do around storage, what they need to do around purchasing stock and so on and so forth.”

Bankers have said a prolonged energy shock could plunge Britain into a recession, while the RAC Foundation said rising petrol prices had already cost drivers hundreds of millions of pounds extra at the pumps.

The Government said on Tuesday night that officials were monitoring the situation, but the UK had a “diverse and resilient energy supply”.

Energy industry sources also cautioned that potential fuel shortages were an unlikely “worst-case scenario” that would only unfold if the US-Israeli war with Iran dragged on into the summer.

Markets have been sent into a panic by the conflict, driving up the price of oil and gas by 40pc and 60pc in the past four weeks.

The situation has been worsened by Iranian threats to strike ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, effectively trapping one-fifth of the world’s oil and LNG supplies in The Gulf.

In Asia, that has triggered a scramble for alternatives – putting countries such as China, Japan and South Korea in competition with Europe for shipments from America, the world’s largest oil and LNG exporter.

Ashley Kelty, an oil and gas analyst at Panmure Liberum, said: “There are lots of American cargoes now being redirected to Asia because they are paying more.”

Households fear looming recession

A senior UK energy industry source acknowledged that the situation described by Shell’s chief executive was “one of the worst-case scenarios but still absolutely possible”.

The source added: “At the moment, people are less worried about the physical security of supply and more about prices.

“Remember, even at the height of the last energy crisis, when we lost huge gas volumes from Europe, we still managed to ensure security of supply.”

But they added: “Clearly there is a point – and it is hard to know exactly when, perhaps June or July – where you get substantial rises in prices and the question becomes whether Europe is actually willing to pay to get it.”

At that point, they said, prices may be so high that households and businesses start to self-ration.

Ministers have powers under the Energy Act to take control of petrol supplies during emergencies. Those powers were previously used in 2000 when striking hauliers blockaded fuel depots.

However, Adam Bell, a former government energy official at consultancy Stonehaven, said he did not think Britain was nearing that situation yet.

Instead, he expects ministers to reach for “softer” demand curbs such as driving speed restrictions or home-working guidance before contemplating any tougher measures.

He added: “If you are going to do anything in the short term, it will be that.”

To combat the energy squeeze, the European Commission has already urged member states to reduce gas storage targets for the coming winter.

Separately on Tuesday, Wall Street bankers said the UK was heading for a recession if the energy price shock continues.

Morgan Stanley said the Bank of England could raise interest rates if energy prices fail to come down, which would be likely to cause an economic downturn by the end of 2026.

Bruna Skarica, the chief UK economist at Morgan Stanley, said Britain would face “a pronounced UK recession at the turn of the year” if energy prices stay at recent highs in the coming months and borrowing costs rise.

Simon French, the chief economist at Panmure Liberum, said a recession in the final six months of the year was now “a real possibility”.

Huw Pill, the chief economist at the Bank of England, also warned that “there are limits to what the MPC [monetary policy committee] can do” to protect households from the energy price shock.

The fallout from the Middle East could also lead to food inflation hitting 8pc by June 2026 if the oil shock persists, according to the Institute of Grocery Distribution. That is more than double the current rate of 3.6pc.

Middle classes may be excluded from support

Rachel Reeves has suggested she will exclude the middle classes from any future support on energy bills.

The Chancellor told the Commons on Tuesday that contingency planning was under way to keep costs down as the conflict in the Middle East continued.

However, she said that she would not roll out an “unfunded, untargeted package of support” similar to the one introduced by the Tories after the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

Household energy bills were capped at £2,500 despite soaring oil and gas prices provoked by Moscow’s war with Kyiv.

Ms Reeves said: “As we respond to this crisis, we must learn from the mistakes of the past.

“The previous government pushed up borrowing, interest rates, inflation and mortgage costs with an unfunded, untargeted package of support under Liz Truss that gave support to the most wealthiest of households.”

Ms Reeves said energy bill support would be “for those who need it most”, adding that she would “update on fuel pricing within the next month”.

The support will reportedly be limited to people on benefits because officials will struggle to develop a system to means-test support for all poorer households in time.

Ms Reeves asked about the potential for establishing an “income threshold” for lower-earning households but has been told such a system will not be ready quickly enough, The Times reported.

HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) records the income of individuals, but energy bill support will have to be targeted at households. A project to link up the information began in January but is set to take more than a year.

The Chancellor is instead likely to rely on eligibility criteria for the Warm Homes Discount, which is limited to those on means-tested benefits.

data.title: Co-op boss steps down after staff criticise ‘toxic culture’
data.description:
The chief executive of the Co-op has resigned just weeks after she was accused of presiding over a “toxic culture” at the business.

Shirine Khoury-Haq has confirmed she will leave the retailer at the end of this week, bringing an end to a turbulent four years in charge, capped off by a major cyber attack last year.

The Co-op said on Thursday that it swung to a £126m loss in 2025 as a result of the breach, compared to a profit of £45m the year prior.

Ms Khoury-Haq said it had “been an honour” to lead the group, with her resignation confirmed just weeks after senior staff wrote a letter to the Co-op board complaining of “fear and alienation”.

Some employees said they felt unable to raise concerns about the direction of the business in front of the leadership team, including Ms Khoury-Haq, according to the letter seen by the BBC.

Lawyers for the Co-op told the BBC they did not recognise the critical comments raised, and “do not believe that they represent the views of our broader leadership and colleagues”.

Ms Khoury-Haq said: “Following last year’s cyber attack, the organisation is now ready to deliver on an ambitious strategy of stabilisation and transformation.

“This extends beyond the timeframe I had planned for my chief executive tenure, and now is the right moment to hand over to leadership that can commit to seeing the strategy through.”

During her tenure, the Beirut-born chief executive had driven a 95pc reduction in Co-op’s debt and a 30pc increase in profit between 2022 and 2024.

She also sought to champion the Co-op’s diversity and inclusion credentials, vowing last year to “protect trans people to the end” after the Supreme Court declared that transgender women were not legally women.

Ms Khoury-Haq wrote to its 54,000 staff in the wake of the ruling, saying she would “continue to advocate” for them and “speak out against hate”.

As well as announcing her departure, Co-op also said on Thursday that the cyber attack last April had delivered a £285m blow to revenues and knocked £107m from profits.

In its latest trading update, bosses said the business would be “recovering in 2026 with market share returning to or surpassing pre-attack levels in every business area”.

The Co-op runs more than 2,300 convenience stores across the UK, and more than 800 funeral parlours. It also runs insurance, legal and wholesale divisions

Chairman Debbie White said: “We thank Shirine for her leadership and for the significant contribution she has made to our Co-op, to our communities and to the co-operative movement during her tenure.

“The board is grateful for her commitment and leadership, particularly during a challenging few years, and we wish her every success in the future.”

Kate Allum, a member-nominated director on the co-operative’s board, will become interim chief executive from Monday while a search is carried out for a permanent successor.

She said it was a “privilege” to take the role “at such an important time for our Co-op”.

“Ours is a business with a clear purpose, a compelling business strategy and a values-led approach to making a positive difference within the communities we serve across the UK,” she said.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: £160m wiped off Currys as boss behind turnaround quits
data.description:
More than £160m was wiped off the value of Currys after the electrical goods giant announced the departure of its chief executive.

Alex Baldock will leave after eight years running the retailer, having spearheaded a successful turnaround and fended off two takeover attempts from foreign suitors.

Confirmation of his exit led to shares in Currys falling by more than 11pc on Thursday, the biggest drop in two years.

Before Thursday’s announcement, shares had risen by more than 160pc in the past three years, with the business now worth £1.33bn.

He has been credited with overseeing a revival in the company’s fortunes after it struggled for years in the wake of a 2014 merger with Carphone Warehouse.

Analysts at City broker Panmure Liberum said Mr Baldock had left Currys a “significantly stronger business than the one he inherited in 2018, when his turnaround strategy was first introduced”.

As well as navigating competition from Amazon and China’s e-commerce giants, he also successfully repelled takeover interest from Chinese online shopping empire JD.com and Wall Street activist investor Elliott in 2024.

Panmure Liberum said Currys had “moved from a net-debt to a net-cash position and restored margins back to pre-Covid levels in the face of tough macroeconomic conditions”.

Ian Dyson, the Currys chairman, said Mr Baldock had “achieved a huge amount” during his tenure, including “transforming the business in the face of some difficult headwinds”.

He added: “Currys is very well positioned for future success with a strategy that is clearly working, great financial health and a very strong leadership team.”

Adam Tomlinson, of German private bank Berenberg, said Mr Baldock had “led the most impressive UK retail turnaround of recent years”.

Mr Baldock is leaving to take an external position but will stay in post while the search for a successor is carried out.

He said: “My eight years at Currys have been simply the best of my career, and I’ll always be a loyal Currys customer, advocate and shareholder.

“Currys’ next chapter can be its most exciting yet. But it’s time for someone else to steer the business there, and time for me to move on to a new opportunity.”

As well as announcing Mr Baldock’s departure, Currys insisted it had traded in line with expectations since January, despite the global turmoil caused by the conflict in the Middle East.

It expects yearly profits to settle between £180m and £190m, an increase of up to 17pc on the previous year.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Disney abandons $1bn deal with OpenAI after it cancels ‘AI slop’ app
data.description:
Disney has cancelled a $1bn (£750m) investment in OpenAI after the tech giant killed its AI video app after just six months.

In an abrupt announcement, the company behind ChatGPT said it would shut down Sora, a service that allowed users to create hyper-realistic AI videos. The decision comes as OpenAI seeks to control its spiralling costs.

The move also sees the end of a $1bn licensing deal with Disney tied to the app, announced just three months ago.

“We’re saying goodbye to the Sora app,” OpenAI said. “To everyone who created with Sora, shared it, and built a community around it: thank you.”

A Disney spokesman said: “We respect OpenAI’s decision to exit the video generation business and to shift its priorities elsewhere.”

The launch of Sora last year was seen as a threat to the modern film industry and acting jobs. Its app also allowed users to use copyrighted characters from the likes of Disney and Nintendo in a casual way.

OpenAI eventually began blocking users from creating copyrighted characters following backlash from Hollywood. In December, it signed a $1bn investment deal with Disney, apparently drawing a line under the dispute.

Disney even said it would allow curated Sora clips on its Disney+ app.

But despite attracting more than 10 million downloads, Sora proved staggeringly expensive, requiring huge amounts of computing power to generate viral videos – dubbed by critics as “AI slop”.

According to one estimate in Forbes, it may have cost as much as $15m per day in computing power alone. Billy Peebles, the head of Sora, said last year: “The economics are currently completely unsustainable.”

It comes as Sam Altman, OpenAI’s chief executive, scrambles to put a lid on soaring costs as he seeks to list the company later this year at a valuation of up to $1tn.

OpenAI has been racing to shore up its finances after pledging to spend as much as $600bn on AI data centres by 2030.

Last month, it announced it had raised $100bn from investors, including Amazon and Nvidia. Yet it has projected it will lose $14bn this year alone.

Despite enjoying huge success with consumers through its ChatGPT app, Mr Altman has been seeking to trim costly side projects and focus on more lucrative business customers.

Anthropic, which develops the rival Claude AI app, has enjoyed glowing reviews from businesses. The rival AI lab has largely avoided launching flashy products aimed solely at consumers and eschewed video and image generation entirely.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Miliband urged to back Shetland gas fields that could fuel UK for five years
data.description:
Britain should move to tap five years’ worth of gas reserves lying near the Shetland Islands, a North Sea energy company has said.

Serica Energy says an estimated five trillion cubic feet of gas lies under the seabed west of Shetland, where there is already infrastructure for piping it back to the mainland.

The claim will pile fresh pressure on Ed Miliband to allow drilling in the North Sea. The Energy Secretary has faced mounting calls to rethink his position on oil and gas amid growing warnings of looming gas shortages linked to the Iran war.

Chris Cox, Serica’s chief executive, said: “Of course [five trillion cubic feet of gas], that sounds like a big number, and it is.

“In fact, it’s equivalent to supplying every household in the UK for five years. And yet, some people continue to say that the amount of gas we can produce in the UK is not significant.”

Serica, which published its 2025 results on Thursday, said its data showed there were many other potential new gas fields in the area.

It follows Wednesday’s Commons clash between Kemi Badenoch and Sir Keir Starmer over Labour’s blocking of two new North Sea oil and gas fields.

Mr Miliband has so far resisted granting approval for plans to exploit the Rosebank field west of Shetland, and Jackdaw in the North Sea. Both are in the final stages of development and could be pouring gas into UK pipes within months.

The fields cited by Serica lie in the same area west of Shetland as Rosebank, some with licences and some without. The latter would fall foul of Mr Miliband’s ban on new exploration and licencing.

Mr Cox said the gas deposits west of Shetland were potentially huge.

“We are very excited by the overall potential,” he said.

Serica has acquired some of the rights while others are held by rival companies such as Adura and Ithaca, whom Mr Cox described as equally “bullish” about the drilling prospects in the area.

Their key advantage is that they lie close enough to existing platforms and pipelines to be developed quickly, meaning they could be in operation within a couple of years. Fuel shortages and price rises caused by the Iran conflict are now predicted to last at least that long.

David Latin, Serica’s chairman, said the Iran conflict showed the need for a rethink by Labour. He called on Mr Miliband to accelerate approvals for licenced fields, lift the ban on new licences and scrap the windfall tax.

He said: “There is significant untapped oil and gas potential on the UK continental shelf … maximising the benefits available to the UK from domestic oil and gas, and achieving net zero by 2050 are not mutually exclusive objectives.

“Indeed, they complement each other, not least when oil and gas imported over thousands of miles typically comes with significantly higher emissions than the equivalent domestic production.”

A government spokesperson argued that the best way for the UK to protect itself was to reduce fossil fuel use, adding: “Issuing new licences to explore new fields cannot give us energy security and will not take a penny off bills.”

Mr Cox warned that the Labour government’s decision to extend the windfall tax by two years to 2030 had hit it hard – leaving the company with an effective overall tax rate of 165pc.

The extension of the windfall tax forced Serica to set aside $130m (£97m), and turned a $80m pre-tax profit into a post tax loss of $52m.

Serica was founded in 2004 and aimed at buying up older fields from larger companies. It is now one of the biggest oil and gas producers in the UK.

Last year its 11 fields produced the equivalent of 118m barrels of oil – half of which was gas that fed into UK pipes.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: British factories brace for an energy crisis worse than the 1970s
data.description:
Europe’s fuel supplies could be squeezed within days, and governments need to act.

That is the ominous warning from Wael Sawan, the boss of Shell boss, who this week said “a ripple effect” could see the continent’s energy security hit “as we get into April”.

A growing number of governments across the world are already introducing exceptional measures to limit the amount of fuel people use as the world reels from the Middle East energy supply shock triggered by war in Iran.

South Korea’s residents have been told to drive less, take shorter showers and use washing machines and vacuum cleaners only on the weekend.

In Sri Lanka, the government has declared that public institutions should take Wednesday off. The island nation has also implemented fuel rationing, with drivers now limited to buying 15 litres of petrol or diesel a day for private cars.

Thailand, with its tropical climate, has called on workers to swap suits for t-shirts in an effort to reduce the use of air conditioning. Swimming pools are no longer being heated in Mauritius and fountains have been turned off at the government’s direction.

All that may seem like a distant concern – something affecting far-off lands with vastly different economies and climates to ours.

Yet restrictions are already beginning to hit Europe. Slovenia became the first EU country this week to introduce fuel rationing, limiting drivers to 50 litres a day.

Here in the UK, ministers insist fuel supplies are secure and are urging people to go about their days as normal. Yet if this crisis drags on, that may not last.

The Telegraph has reported that strict speed limits could be imposed under the National Emergency Plan for Fuel, should ministers choose to activate it.

Adam Bell, a former government energy official at consultancy Stonehaven, said home-working guidance may also be likely if the Government decides to bring in measures to curb energy consumption in the coming months.

“If you are going to do anything in the short term, it will be that,” he says.

“If you do not have enough fuel, governments have to think very carefully about how they use the fuel they have, what is still coming in, and what they hold in inventories,” says Callum MacPherson, the head of commodities at Investec.

Ashley Kelty, of investment bank Panmure Liberum, said limits on the amount of gas factories can use may also be considered.

Even if the Government doesn’t step in, manufacturers may adopt self-imposed limits as costs skyrocket.

The average annual electricity bill for small industrial and commercial businesses has risen by 20pc, or £96,000, to £578,000 since the war in Iran began, figures from Cornwall Insight show.

For those businesses that use gas – including chemical producers, glass and ceramics factories and metalworks – the pain is even worse. Average annual gas bills have risen by nearly 60pc to just more than £1m.

“Many of these companies are already battling slimmer margins, so this rise in energy costs is not something they can simply absorb,” Jacob Briggs, from Cornwall Insight, says.

“For some firms, this could mean the difference between investing in growth this year or shelving their plans entirely, and for others, high bills could force some very difficult economic decisions.”

Arjan Geveke, the director of the Energy Intensive Users Group, told The Telegraph last week there was a “high risk” that factories would start voluntarily cutting production in the coming weeks because of high energy costs.

Kelty fears that the impact of the crisis on the UK economy could be “horrendous”, with the automotive and steel manufacturing sectors the worst affected.

He said: “Even if the rockets stop tomorrow, the problem is that it’s going to take a month to get back to any form of normality in terms of restarting oil production and getting all the tankers out of the Gulf and to their original destinations.”

The UK will face a “very challenging” winter, as even if the conflict comes to an end, it will struggle to refill its gas storage, Kelty added.

All this is set to weigh on the economy. If the energy crisis continues and Brent crude oil remains at around $100 a barrel, Capital Economics has forecast that the UK will enter a mild recession by the end of the year.

Jennifer McKeown, chief global economist from Capital Economics, said: “The energy-intensive sectors of industry, hotels and retail are likely to be hit the hardest.

“The sharp increase in the cost of energy is going to mean that it is quite likely that industrial production will need to be cut back.”

In the summer of 2022, as energy prices soared following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, many European nations imposed policies to curb oil and gas consumption.

Measures at the time included restrictions on air conditioning and reduced lighting for national monuments and advertising boards.

By late 2022, Britons were encouraged to cut energy use during peak times, with some electricity providers offering reduced costs in an effort to encourage households to shift consumption to off-peak hours.

Restrictions were far more draconian during the Arab oil crisis of the 1970s. In 1973, drivers were using fuel ration books and were advised to reduce their maximum speed to 50mph.

By early 1974, fuel shortages and rationing meant that many industrial businesses were operating with a three-day working week and TV channels were shut off after 10.30pm.

Fatih Birol, executive director of the International Energy Agency (IEA), has said that the current energy supply shock is worse than the 1970s. “No country will be immune to the effects of this crisis if it continues to go in this direction,” he has warned. “So there is a need for global efforts.”

The IEA has called for car-free Sundays globally and urged people to work from home three days a week in order to conserve fuel.

Ministers in Britain are understood to have found this intervention unhelpful. With the Government convinced the UK has enough fuel for the moment, their biggest concern is to avoid panic buying or unnecessary changes in business patterns that could create fresh problems.

However, disruption to energy supplies looks likely to continue. QatarEnergy, which operates the world’s largest liquefied natural gas facility, said on Tuesday that it was preparing to declare “force ‌majeure” on some fuel contracts.

The crisis measure would allow it to stop shipments agreed under contract without facing any legal liability. Italy, Belgium, South Korea ⁠and China will be affected.

MacPherson said: “If this went on and on, and we had to live in a world where there is much less oil than we have been used to in the past, people would have to travel less, and when they do travel, it would be more expensive.”

While Britain may have escaped the worst of it so far, the threat of an energy-triggered economic crunch looms.

data.title: Heathrow’s new car parks to cost more than Gatwick’s second runway
data.description:
From HS2 to Crossrail, British infrastructure projects lead the world in one unenviable respect: their sizeable, often runaway budgets.

The bill for big-ticket construction schemes typically starts off with eye-watering sums – and then climb to even more staggering levels as budgets are blown and costs run rampant.

Now a plan to provide 36,000 parking spaces at Heathrow airport is poised to join the pantheon of cash-hungry mega-projects after it was revealed that it will come with a staggering £2.6bn price tag.

That’s £400m more than the estimated outlay for a new runway at Gatwick and more than half the cost of Heathrow’s Terminal 5 – the largest free-standing structure in the UK.

At £72,000 per parking space, the two multi-storey car parks planned under the scheme will also be comfortably the most expensive in Britain and a contender for the costliest ever built.

Heathrow argues the price per space will be lower because only half the overall cost, about £1.3bn, is for the buildings themselves, with the rest of the spending going towards associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Yet the airport has faced criticism from some of the world’s largest airlines, who will ultimately pick up the bill for the world’s most expensive car park.

In a week when National Car Parks (NCP), the country’s biggest car park operator, collapsed into administration, the plan might seem an ambitious one.

However, unlike NCP, which was hit by a drop in commuting, Heathrow can rely on a captive customer base and funding rules that mean construction costs are ultimately likely to be passed on as aircraft landing fees.

Airline bosses have attacked the project as typical of Heathrow’s largesse in lavishing cash on gold-plated enhancements that they and their customers must ultimately pay for.

The airport says the car parks will be a vital component of its £49bn plan for a third runway that will lift passenger numbers from 84 million a year to as many as 150 million.

It says the sites – one next to the M4 and the other to the south near the M25 – will be needed to cope with the thousands of additional passengers driving to what is already Europe’s busiest airport each day, even if a higher proportion of people take public transport.

And it argues that the expense of the project reflects construction costs in London that are themselves amongst the highest in the world.

But Luis Gallego, the boss of British Airways (BA) owner International Airlines Group, said the plan was overpriced.

He highlighted the shortcomings of a funding model that allows Heathrow to charge airlines a regulated fee to cover building and operating costs.

“We support expansion, but not at any cost. All future spending must be carefully controlled as Heathrow already charges passengers twice as much as Amsterdam and three times as much as Paris,” he said.

Willie Walsh, the director general of the International Air Transport Association, was blunter, describing the car parks plan as “madness” and saying his mind “boggled” at the £2.6bn bill.

He said Heathrow had proposed what would have been the world’s most expensive car park when he was chief executive of BA as part of a previous runway plan that was rejected in 2010.

He said: “You can find data on what it costs to build a car park, there’s lots of evidence there, and Heathrow is just way out of line with the rest of the world.

“The problem is that they are incentivised to spend as much money as possible and to build the most expensive car park and to be proud of that.”

Heathrow has said that calling the new structures car parks does not do them justice.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, has instead referred to them as “parking villages”.

The blueprint for the new runway proposes “two large parkway car parks” that would replace more than 20 existing facilities, with the northern site able to accommodate 20,000 vehicles and the southern one 16,000.

It adds: “This could support fast and efficient connections to each terminal campus via dedicated transport systems.

“These new parkways could also provide local communities with improved access to airport terminals and public transport links and help reduce congestion on local roads.”

While a leaked Heathrow briefing paper states the cost of the parkways to be £2.6bn, a spokesman said the parking areas alone will consume only about half the budget, with the rest covering the cost of associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Major profit centre

The car parks may be necessary to cope with an influx of new passengers, but they would also be a major profit centre, with parking fees contributing £200m in revenue last year.

Heathrow’s long-stay car parks currently charge £46.80 for the first day and £37.40 for each additional 24-hour period.

Taking an average of £40 per day, the number of spaces planned could generate more than £500m in revenue over the course of a year.

That is not including income from drop-off charges and the short-stay car parks nearer to terminals that the airport has indicated it plans to retain.

Michael O’Leary, the chief executive of Ryanair, which does not fly from Heathrow, said drop-off fees were “a scandal” across UK airports and that parking had been turned into a cash cow integral to their business models.

He said: “The more they earn from car parks the more they will dividend it out to their shareholders and then complain that they can’t make a reasonable return on the risks they were taking investing in runways in London.

“But there’s no risk at all, it’s a complete freebie, which is why pension funds now own most of these airports.”

O’Leary said the cost of runways, car parks and terminals was ultimately the result of a “broken” regulatory model that “rewards inefficiency and incentivises airports to squander gazillions”.

A study from Oxford Global Projects commissioned by Heathrow Reimagined, a lobby group formed by airlines including BA, has suggested that Heathrow’s construction costs across a variety of infrastructure including car parks are consistently among the highest in the world.

The report said the cost of the runway alone – covering cement, asphalt and lights – would come in at more than £800,000 per metre, making it one of the most expensive ever built.

Jonathan Simcock, author of The Delivery Gap, an examination of failed infrastructure projects, said companies like Heathrow have an incentive to inflate estimated costs by “including all the bells and whistles they can”.

In so doing, he said, they aim to secure regulatory approval for levels of spending – in Heathrow’s case to be funded by airlines and their customers – that will cover all eventualities and “ensure they are not left holding the baby”.

“If the estimate is inflated then the company ends up making an inappropriate profit. But if it’s undercooked the company gets into trouble, somebody has to bail them out and the country doesn’t get the infrastructure it needs,” he says.

Woldbye has said that the costings for the car parks and the wider runway project could change and that Heathrow is open to conversations about modifying its plans.

However, Simcock warned: “When the conversation is about how it can possibly cost that much, very often it turns out that it should have been, can you really do it for that?

“For very large and highly complex HS2-type things – and Heathrow is big and complex – it is rare to get nice surprises and much more common to get nasty ones.”
