data.title: Water boss accused of potential conflict of interest over lobbying ties
data.description:
The chairman of a water quango that sticks up for consumers has been accused of a potential conflict of interest because of his stake in a lobbying firm that works with utilities.

Rob Wilson, who runs the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), is still listed as a senior adviser at Political Insight UK, The Telegraph can reveal.

Mr Wilson, a former Conservative minister under David Cameron, co-founded the firm and is a minority shareholder.

Founded in 2020, Political Insight UK claims on its website to help clients with “government relations” by advising them on “policy development and regulatory issues”.

This includes clients across a range of sectors, such as transport, housing, retail and public utilities. The website makes no mention of individual water companies, though the term utilities covers the water industry.

His links to the lobbying firm have raised concern owing to his role at CCW, a government-approved quango funded by households and businesses.

The CCW brands itself as the voice of billpayers by holding companies, government and regulators to account.

Alistair Carmichael, chairman of the environment, food and rural affairs committee, said: “Public confidence in the water industry is already at an all-time low.

“A body like the CCW has to be seen to be the consumers’ voice and nothing else. This is not a moment where anyone can be seen to be riding two horses.”

After publication, Mr Wilson claimed that he had not undertaken any client advisory work for the firm for more than two years, and none at all since his appointment as CCW chairman. He said the website was outdated.

He also stressed that before that, he did not advise any clients in the public utilities sector. He denies that his stake in Political Insight gives rise to any conflict of interest regarding his role at the CCW.

The row will heighten pressure on Mr Wilson after a cache of receipts revealed how he twice claimed discounts on train tickets last year by using a railcard for students.

On one occasion, he was caught by a conductor and asked to pay a £16.40 fare correction, which he later expensed.

The CCW said Mr Wilson had clicked the 16-25 railcard by accident and made the mistake in “good faith”.

Mr Wilson previously drew scrutiny during his time as a Tory MP for claiming 9p for a 320-metre car journey.

As well as owning a stake in Political Insight UK, the former minister is also a director for Transport Focus, a consumer action group for rail passengers.

In recent years, Transport Focus has worked in tandem with government officials to combat fare evasion.

The CCW says on its website that Mr Wilson, who was appointed chairman by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) last year, has a “number of shareholdings declared”.

However, the group said that “the list does not include any water companies”.

‘Radical transparency’

A CCW spokesman said the conflict of interest claims were a matter for Defra.

Defra asks public appointees to disclose any potential conflicts of interest to the department and puts mitigation measures in place if necessary.

Speaking last year, Mr Wilson called for “radical transparency” and greater accountability to rebuild trust in the water sector.

He also spoke to water companies about how to address whether their actions are acceptable at a conference.

“If this were explained on the front page of tomorrow’s newspaper, would the public see it as fair, responsible and in their interest?” he said, addressing attendees in his capacity as CCW chairman.

“If the answer is yes, you are rebuilding confidence. If the answer is no, you are eroding it.”

Scrutiny of Mr Wilson’s lobbying ties comes at an awkward time for the CCW.

The group has so far failed to publish financial accounts as it battles with the National Audit Office (NAO) amid a dispute over its expenditure.

Analysis of the CCW’s board minutes from last year also highlights how the group is in a separate spat with HMRC.

A CCW spokesman said this matter concerned a decision not to include VAT on its sales invoices.

“After extensive communication with HMRC, we have now become VAT registered to allow us to correct the issue, which we are in the process of doing,” the spokesman said.

“Having revisited the calculations HMRC provides, we understand any resulting interest and surcharges will be minimal.”

Defra was contacted for comment.

data.title: Housebuilder facing cash squeeze handed £300m by government
data.description:
Labour is under pressure to explain why the Government has handed a troubled housebuilder more than £300m in taxpayer-backed loans and contracts.

Paul Holmes, the shadow housing minister, said there were “questions about Labour’s judgement” after it partnered with Vistry to deliver affordable housing.

The London-listed building firm has been handed around £315m in grants and loans, according to Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister.

Homes England, the housing quango responsible for allocating the funds, does not directly monitor the financial viability of the companies it works with, he added.

Shares in Vistry have crashed by more than 50pc over the past month following the surprise departure of Greg Fitzgerald, chief executive, and amid concerns about a cash squeeze at the business.

Vistry has announced plans to offer deep discounts on some of its homes to offload them and raise capital. Reports suggest it has also demanded delays on paying subcontractors and asked for discounts as it battles to preserve cash.

It follows a string of profit warnings after bosses underestimated the cost of building homes in the south of England.

Paul Holmes, the Conservative shadow Housing minister, submitted an official parliamentary question to Mr Pennycook last week on the taxpayer’s “financial commitment to Vistry”.

“Vistry’s plight raises serious doubts about Labour’s ability to deliver their so-called Affordable Homes Programme,” Mr Holmes told The Telegraph.

“And it also raises questions about Labour’s judgement. If the Government isn’t properly scrutinising the companies it partners with, how can it say whether they will succeed or fail?

“Labour must come clean about how and why ministers concluded Vistry was the best choice – and tell us how much taxpayers’ money could be lost.”

In November, ministers cleared Vistry to bid for as much as £700m in taxpayer subsidies as part of the Government’s £39bn affordable homes programme. It has the same status under the existing programme, begun by the Conservatives in 2021.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said there were “amber flags” about Vistry’s financial position.

“Vistry needs to slow down, shrink the business, stabilise the cash situation before it sees growth,” he said.

Mr Codling added that the going concern note in Vistry’s latest financial accounts had grown from around 350 words to more than 1,700 words, three times the size that it was this time last year, which “paints a darker downside” than before.

The wider economic backdrop also offers little comfort, with housebuilders warning of inflationary uncertainty stemming from the conflict in Iran.

“If the market really turns down, it’ll get a lot trickier for Vistry,” said Aynsley Lammin of Investec.

Mr Pennycook said that the Affordable Homes Programme does not carry hard credit risk exposure on companies it works with, as it is a grant programme, so a “purposely high-level review” is undertaken instead.

The ministers added that there are “ no checks undertaken on the off-balance sheet liabilities” as it “is not the role of Homes England to undertake liquidity tests on strategic partners”.

A Vistry spokesman said that taxpayer money was not at risk and said it “has been deployed on building affordable homes that this country desperately needs”.

They added that Vistry “continues to maintain a comfortable level of headroom against its banking facilities”.

“We work closely with Homes England on the delivery of new homes, and, like all of its partners, our programme is closely monitored and audited,” they said.

“The grant funding has all been committed, and the homes for which the funds were allocated are now contracted and owned by regulated, registered providers.”

In March, Mr Fitzgerald vowed to shrink Vistry’s debt by the end of this year.

A spokesman for Homes England said: “Homes England operates within robust, transparent frameworks when working with delivery partners. We don’t comment on the commercial or financial position of individual partners.”

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The green-belt village about to fall under the shadow of Europe’s largest data centre
data.description:
“The peace, quiet and openness of the country.” That’s why Danny Leach and his family first moved to North Ockendon, on the north-east side of Greater London, back in 2005.

Despite being barely outside the M25 and a part of the London Borough of Havering, the village and surrounding countryside are remarkably quiet.

The fenland surrounding the village is undeveloped and, considering its proximity to the capital, surprisingly biodiverse.

“I’ve got nearly 100 species of birds that visit my garden, including kingfishers, barn owls and tawny owls,” says Leach. “There are also great crested newts and foxes. On a summer evening, I can hear the frogs croaking.”

Part of the reason such an environment can exist so close to a city of 10 million is the Metropolitan Green Belt, which, since its creation in 1938, has protected the countryside near London and prevented urban sprawl.

Now, a proposed Green Belt-busting development could change the area’s character forever.

Havering council recently launched a consultation on “East Havering Data Centre”, a hyperscale AI facility that would make North Ockendon home to the largest data centre in Europe.

The facility would use 540 acres of existing farmland and require 600 megawatts of electricity to operate – the same amount of energy needed to run one million homes.

Developer Digital Reef says the site will include around 84 acres of data centre buildings, along with a 280-acre ecological park.

Residents were outraged to learn that the consultation period for the 2,700-page proposal would last only 30 days, clashing with the May elections, and that Havering council were processing the project via a “Local Development Order”, a special type of planning tool that allows conventional planning permission steps to be skipped.

Labour and Conservative members of the council’s strategic planning committee advocated a longer, 12-week consultation period, but were outvoted by the Havering Residents Association, a local party that runs the council.

Data centres are essentially large warehouses filled with specialist computing equipment that processes AI requests submitted by users on their phones or computers. East Havering Data Centre is one of hundreds of these projects proposed near Britain’s major cities to power the growing demand for AI, many of which will be built on green-belt land.

Data centres require large amounts of energy and water to operate, raising concerns that construction plans could put pressure on electricity and water infrastructure and drive up household bills.

Leach is one of many North Ockendon residents who are unhappy with the proposal. His property, which backs on to the site, will be particularly affected.

‘People move to the country for a reason’

“It’s a big shock,” he says. “People move to the countryside for a reason, and then suddenly you have something the size of a battleship going up at the end of your garden.”

Local businesses are also worried. Daniel Anthony has run Top Meadow Golf Club his entire working life, taking over from his father.

Since launching a nine-hole golf club in 1985, the family has grown the business into an 18-hole club and 20-room hotel that employs 50 people.

“Everything, everything, everything has been reinvested from day one,” says Anthony. He is worried the project is putting a lifetime of work at risk.

Today, Anthony says the club makes most of its money from functions hosted at the hotel and restaurant: “We do anything from birthdays and anniversaries to funerals, which are a big one for us because it’s only two and a half miles to the nearest crematorium.”

East Havering Data Centre will be built directly across the road from Top Meadow. Anthony is worried that the facility will be a blight on the club’s prized views of the countryside. “It’s the view really; that’s what people come for,” he says.

He is also concerned about industrial traffic and possible traffic diversions during the construction. A traffic diversion could extend the journey from Top Meadow to the nearby crematorium from two and a half to 10 miles, threatening the club’s funeral and wake business.

The rise of data centres

Developer Digital Reef estimates the facility will take four years to construct – longer than most data centre projects.

Ed Griffiths, of construction consultancy Barbour ABI, says construction is relatively fast for most data centres, with build programmes averaging 19 to 30 months, depending on scale and power requirements.

Sites near large cities are ideal for AI data centres, as user requests can be processed more quickly.

“Urban fringe locations are common for data centres, particularly where they provide proximity to users while still offering enough space and infrastructure to support large facilities,” says Griffiths.

This makes the green-belt land that surrounds many of Britain’s cities tempting for developers.

Data provided to The Telegraph by Barbour ABI indicate that 13 of the 100 largest data centres planned in the UK would fall within the green belt.

These green-belt data centres have a combined value of just over £10bn, representing an outsized share of the 100 largest planned projects at 19pc of the £52bn total.

Data centre construction is a priority for the Government, which views the facilities as essential for turning Britain into an “AI superpower” and for growing the economy.

In a January 2025 speech, Sir Keir Starmer said AI will be “mainlined into the veins of the nation” and has classed data centres as “critical national infrastructure”.

‘Destroying a rich ecosystem’

Many developers include environmental concessions, such as the East Havering Data Centre’s “ecology park” in London, in their planning applications as a sweetener for nearby communities.

However, Ian Pirie, a Havering Friends of the Earth co-ordinator, is sceptical of their benefits.

“Developers think that they can just tear up all that land and then dig somewhere else, plant some trees, and they’ve restored the ecosystem. It’s complete nonsense,” says Pirie.

“They are destroying an already existing, rich ecosystem. It would take another 100 years to have what we’ve got there now.”

Another local concern is the data centre’s potential water usage. The facilities often use water cooling to prevent computers from overheating.

Digital Reef has proposed that excess heat generated by the data centre will be redirected to heat local homes and a novel 12-acre indoor avocado farm.

But residents are unconvinced, telling The Telegraph that the local water supply is already temperamental and that industrial usage could push it to breaking point.

A spokesman for North Ockendon Residents’ Association says: “The water supply here is very old, under-maintained pipe work. If they were to then start taking thousands of gallons a day and something happened, we would be without water.”

Griffiths, who works at Barbour ABI, says access to grid power is the most important factor in selecting a site for a data centre, followed by water availability.

Data centres’ use of energy and water resources could have a national impact. Ireland, which is a hub for trans-Atlantic data cables and hosts the European headquarters of American tech giants such as Amazon and Google, has prioritised its data centre infrastructure.

In 2024, Ireland’s data centres used 22pc of its total electricity supply, more than the 18pc used by the country’s urban homes, according to the Central Statistics Office. There are concerns that a similar build-out in the UK would drive up energy costs, squeezing consumers.

A spokesman for Havering Local Planning Authority says that a full evidence base for East Havering Data Centre would be considered as part of the application, including assessments on biodiversity, ecology, economic impact, transport, water usage and use of the green belt. 

Digital Reef could not be reached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”

data.title: Britain’s high streets ‘dragged down by vape shops’
data.description:
Britain’s high streets are being dragged down by a proliferation of vape shops and empty stores, the Communities Secretary has said.

Steve Reed said the decline of town centres had eroded pride in local areas as he vowed that the Government would bring “high streets back to life” by reopening shops and supporting businesses.

It comes as ministers unveiled a £301m package for councils to spend on revitalising town centres by investing in local businesses, building new homes, libraries and parks.

‘A kind of quiet patriotism’

Speaking to The Telegraph, Mr Reed said: “For most people, what they see when they step outside their front door sets the tone for their day and often their life.

“And when the town where you live feels run-down, it drags everything down.

“Half the shops shut and the rest struggling or selling vapes. A playground with broken swings nobody’s bothered to fix. A bus shelter with broken glass that has been like that for years.

“But you also see people who care deeply about where they live and deserve a government that cares too.

“This week we’re showing them we do, putting £301m bringing high streets back to life – reopening shops and backing local businesses.

“This goes far beyond just how places look. It’s about whether people feel they belong, can take pride in where they live, and see a decent future for themselves and their family. 

“As Sunday Telegraph readers will recognise, that pride is a kind of classic patriotism: quiet, unshowy, rooted in wanting your town to be somewhere you want to call home.”

Mr Reed said a further £18m had been allocated to restore and update children’s playgrounds in the most deprived parts of Britain.

The investment is part of the Government’s Pride in Place initiative, which ministers hope will arrest Labour’s slide in the polls ahead of the local elections on May 7.

The party has slumped to third in the latest YouGov weekly voting intention polls on 17 per cent, two points behind the Greens and eight points behind Reform UK.

Mr Reed has vowed to target vape shops and US-style sweet shops in his role as Communities Secretary.

He previously said people were “sick to death” of seeing those shops taking over high streets, some of which “turn out to be fronts for organised crime”.

His comments come after a study warned that Labour would be “washed away in a tide of discontent” at the next general election unless it tackled the decline of high streets.

Research by the University of Southampton found that people felt high streets had declined more than any other part of their local area over the past decade as well-known retailers collapsed and shoplifting rose.

Last week, The Telegraph revealed that the Chancellor Rachel Reeves’s business rates increase next month will see high street convenience shops face an average rise of £1,600 over the next two years.

Sir Mel Stride, shadow chancellor, said Ms Reeves’s actions risked undermining Britain as a “nation of shopkeepers”.

This is about restoring pride in where you live

By Steve Reed

For most people, what they see when they step outside their front door sets the tone for their day and often their life.

And when the town where you live feels run-down, it drags everything down.

Half the shops shut and the rest struggling or selling vapes. A playground with broken swings nobody’s bothered to fix. A bus shelter with broken glass that has been like that for years.

But you also see people who care deeply about where they live and deserve a government that cares too.

This week we’re showing them we do, putting £301m bringing high streets back to life – reopening shops and backing local businesses.

This goes far beyond just how places look. It’s about whether people feel they belong, can take pride in where they live, and see a decent future for themselves and their family. 

‘Fixes for things that matter’

As Sunday Telegraph readers will recognise, that pride is a kind of classic patriotism: quiet, unshowy, rooted in wanting your town to be somewhere you want to call home.

Places matter. They always have. But for too long successive Tory governments let them to go to the wall.

So, we’re investing £18m to repair and replace playgrounds in 66 deprived communities, from Tyneside to Torquay.

We’re also backing the first Pride in Place projects with up to £20m over a decade. That’s £5.8bn going straight into 284 communities across the UK, with initial investment plans already approved and ready to deliver. Money for neighbourhoods that need it most, whose residents will decide how it’s spent.

These aren’t trophy schemes or glossy buildings – they’re fixes for the things that really matter and make everyday lives better.

Some people like to say nothing can change and the system is beyond repair.

That’s not patriotism. It’s giving up while pretending to tell hard truths.

Real patriotism – the kind I believe in – means backing the country as it actually is: in the streets, parks and town centres people use every day.

It’s about reversing decades of neglect in places promised the world and given nothing. “Levelling up”. “Taking back control”. The slogans came and went. The broken swings stayed broken.

The money we’re putting into playgrounds matters because no child should miss out on the joy of playing outdoors with their friends.

‘It’s about people power’

In the 21st century, town centres thrive when they have a proper mix of life – homes, health centres, libraries, community hubs and green spaces alongside shops.

As part of our High Streets Strategy, we are investing in making our high streets fit for the future and we’ll confirm the first locations in the coming weeks.

For us, it’s about whether places feel looked after and right for their residents. Can you buy essentials on your doorstep? Do you have any real power to improve your neighbourhood – to take on the fly-tipping, the graffiti, that broken pavement?

This is what our £5.8 billion Pride in Place programme is all about – people power. And I’m delighted to see the exciting first projects we’ve just confirmed are going ahead.

In Spennymoor, Co Durham, they’re setting up a young people’s fund. In Dewsbury, West Yorkshire they’re creating a prevention team to tackle crime and anti-social behaviour.

In Greenock, Scotland a new Enterprise Hub will support local start-up businesses. That’s change in action.

For too long, decisions were taken at a distance and services built around bureaucracy. The era of the top-down state must end. People want – and deserve – a real say in how their schools, surgeries and job centres work.

Money and decisions pushed closest to the people who know what needs changing always go further.

And this is only the beginning.

The new generation of new towns we’ve committed to building will mean more homes, more affordable homes, more jobs, better transport and they’ll be greener – a turning point in how we create thriving communities.

We’ll be saying more about this very soon – that’s the next chapter.

Street by street, neighbourhood by neighbourhood, we’re backing people with the power to shape their own futures.

Steve Reed is the MP for Streatham and Croydon North and the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government

data.title: Virgin Media O2 threatens to stop building broadband if £2bn deal blocked
data.description:
The co-owner of Virgin Media O2 (VMO2) has threatened to stop building broadband in the UK if the telecoms giant’s £2bn takeover of Netomnia is blocked.

Mike Fries, the chief executive of Liberty Global, which owns VMO2 alongside Telefónica, warned he could pull the plug on investment if regulators give in to criticism by blocking the deal to buy their rival.

The complex transaction, agreed last month, will see Liberty Global and Telefónica team up with InfraVia, a French private equity firm, to buy Netomnia through their joint venture Nexfibre.

The tie-up will bolster VMO2’s position as the main challenger to incumbent BT, creating a combined full-fibre network reaching roughly eight million homes.

But the deal is facing fierce opposition from CityFibre, the UK’s third-largest broadband provider, which has urged regulators to intervene based on the claim that it will “significantly reduce competition”.

Speaking at a New Street Research conference in London on Tuesday, Mr Fries said: “There’s no theory of harm here that stacks up, and I can tell you all the counterfactuals are bad.

“Maybe we stop building fibre, or Nexfibre stops building fibre ... the only counterfactual that’s certain is BT gets stronger.”

VMO2’s takeover of Netomnia has been viewed by some industry observers as the trigger for a wave of consolidation across Britain’s turbulent broadband market.

A wave of smaller firms, known as “altnets”, has cropped up to challenge BT’s dominance. However, rising interest rates and weaker-than-expected uptake have left many struggling to survive.

London-focused G Network collapsed into administration in January after being bought by vulture capital firm FitzWalter, which will now seek a buyer after stripping the company of its debts.

Mr Fries called complaints about the Netomnia deal “sour grapes”, adding: “CityFibre was trying to do the same deal so I don’t see how it’s a different outcome for Nexfibre versus CityFibre.”

CityFibre has previously called for consolidation in the altnet market, and was also exploring a potential bid for Netomnia.

However, it is thought that the debt-laden company, which is backed by Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund Mubadala and Goldman Sachs, would need to raise more money to finance any deals.

Speaking at the same event, Nick Dunn, the CityFibre finance chief, insisted that his company would have other takeover opportunities if VMO2’s Netomnia swoop goes ahead.

The Netomnia deal is widely expected to face an initial phase-one investigation by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA). It could also be referred to a more in-depth review.

Mr Fries urged regulators to approve the deal after phase one, arguing that blocking the transaction would undermine Labour’s pledge to kickstart economic growth.

The chief executive said: “The Government has talked about transforming regulation in this country, working at pace, not stifling innovation and growth. This is a test case for that.”

He added: “Who is going to build a scale-based competitor to BT Openreach? Who has the capital, the wherewithal, the customers? I don’t think there’s anybody else. The Government should see this as a net positive for sure.”

A CityFibre spokesman said: “For 20 years, VMO2 has failed to challenge BT’s dominance of the market. It’s only now that competition is emerging to both of those players, for the benefit of consumers and the UK. 

“The proposed deal would reduce choice for millions of homes and weaken the competitive pressure that has finally begun to transform the market. We have no doubt the CMA will do a thorough job of investigating the transaction.”

data.title: HBO Max launches in UK under a cloud of uncertainty
data.description:
Steve Carell and Lisa Kudrow were among the stars hobnobbing with media executives on Wednesday night as they celebrated the launch of the UK’s latest streaming service at a glamorous bash at London’s Southbank Centre.

HBO Max, which rolls out on Thursday, hopes to set itself apart from the deluge of modern TV options by offering a premium service to its subscribers.

From hit HBO series such as Succession, The White Lotus and The Sopranos to Warner Bros blockbusters including recent Oscar winners One Battle After Another and Sinners, executives are keen to stress the platform’s status as the home of prestige viewing.

For an extra fee, subscribers will also be able to watch TNT Sports, which has the rights to some Premier League and FA Cup football matches.

The US media giant is hoping to create a streaming service that thrives on “Fomo” – the fear of missing out.

“You’re just not going to want to miss what’s on HBO Max,” JB Perrette, global head of streaming at HBO parent company Warner Bros, told The Telegraph.

Yet beneath the hype is a more turbulent reality, as the UK launch comes amid uncertainty about Warner Bros’ future under Paramount, soon to be its new owner.

When Warner Bros first announced the UK launch of HBO Max just a month ago, it had agreed to an $82bn (£62bn) takeover by Netflix. At the time, Mr Perrette lauded Netflix as having an “enormous amount of appreciation [and] respect” for the HBO brand.

Then Paramount, the Hollywood studio controlled by Oracle billionaire Larry Ellison, derailed Netflix’s swoop by tabling a winning $111bn offer.

As a result, the future of HBO Max, which starts at £4.99 per month but rises to £34.99 for its most expensive plan, is already in question. David Ellison, the son of Larry Ellison and chief executive of Paramount, has outlined plans to fold HBO Max into the Paramount+ streaming service.

Mr Perrette insists that Paramount is just as supportive of HBO as Netflix. “Price alone would tell you they value it significantly,” he says. He adds that it would be “crazy” to imagine a future in which the HBO brand does not survive in some form.

Yet fundamentally, Warner Bros executives cannot be sure whether the UK’s newest streaming platform will still exist as a standalone service in a year’s time.

“It’s an incredibly exciting thing, but exactly what that means and how it comes to market, I think, is still to be written,” says Mr Perrette.

Andrew Georgiou, who leads Warner Bros in the UK and Ireland, adds: “We’re just trying to launch the best possible products so that, whenever they [Paramount] come to own this business, they’ve got the best starting point to do what they want.”

Perhaps more significant is the prospect of editorial intervention by the Ellisons, who have close ties to Donald Trump. Paramount has already been accused of shifting its news network CBS to the Right, while the studio is reportedly working on a new instalment of the Rush Hour franchise following a personal request from the US president.

The Warner Bros executives declined to comment on the possibility of political interference. They also said it was too early to know how a $6bn cost-cutting programme planned by the Ellisons would affect the UK business.

Instead, HBO hopes it can cut through the noise and distractions with a slate of high-quality programming. The Pitt, an Emmy award-winning US medical drama starring Noah Wyle, is being released to coincide with the launch.

The upcoming Harry Potter TV series, scheduled to be released in time for Christmas, will most likely win over British audiences.

But as Warner Bros gears up for a $111bn takeover that will reshape Hollywood, bosses will be hoping their new streaming service survives long enough to see in the return of the wizarding world.

data.title: Bank founded by Mandelson ally fined £2m for ‘fabricated documents’
data.description:
A bank founded by a top Labour donor has been fined £2m for faking documents in an effort to mislead the City watchdog about its finances.

The Bank of London, founded by Anthony Watson, provided “several fabricated documents” to the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) to create a “false picture of the capital position”.

In a decision handed down on Tuesday, the PRA fined the Bank of London, which previously counted Lord Mandelson as a board member, for “failing to act with integrity”.

This followed an investigation into failings at the bank between October 2021 and May 2024.

An investigation by the PRA, part of the Bank of England, found that the company failed to inform officials it was at risk of insolvency between December 2023 and May 2024.

That was despite being just seven days from running out of cash during this period.

The PRA said it was the first time it had been forced to fine a bank for a lack of integrity.

It said an initial £12m fine had been reduced to £2m based on the fact that it would pose “serious financial hardship” to the business.

Mr Watson, a former Barclays and Nike executive and a corporate LGBT champion, launched the Bank of London in 2021 to shake up the clearing banking sector, which handles the back-end transactions between lenders.

The start-up bank raised tens of millions of pounds and at one point was valued at $1.1bn (£822.5bn).

This led to Mr Watson hiring a high-profile board, including Lord Mandelson, the former business secretary and Harvey Schwartz, the chief executive of private equity giant Carlyle. However, the bank quickly encountered financial difficulties.

History of financial troubles

According to the PRA, the Bank of London was struggling to meet its capital requirements by January 2023.

To ease regulatory concerns, executives sent emails to the PRA, supposedly from their lawyers, claiming £1m had been wired to the business.

The company was running short on cash again by December, at which point it failed to alert the City watchdog.

Executives also wrongly recognised £4.7m as revenues to make its accounts appear healthier than they actually were.

The Bank of London was later bailed out by its investors, with US fund Mangrove Capital Partners taking control of the business and installing a new leadership team.

Mr Watson, who has previously given hundreds of thousands of pounds to top Labour MPs, left the bank in September 2024.

At the time, he said: “As difficult as it is for any founder-CEO to step back, I know in my heart that this is the right time for me.”

The Telegraph contacted Mr Watson for comment.

Turmoil at the bank first came to light when it was issued with a winding-up petition by the taxman in September 2024. Lord Mandelson subsequently left the business as part of a boardroom clear-out.

After the PRA’s ruling, a spokesman for the Bank of London said: “The matters described in the notice relate to a period when the Bank was under previous ownership and management.

“The bank accepts the PRA’s findings and regrets the failings identified.

“The bank has been implementing a comprehensive remediation programme, and is continuing work to strengthen further its governance and risk management arrangements and its financial and regulatory reporting controls.”

The spokesman said it plans to return to growth this year.

data.title: Meta trial should be ‘pivot point’ for big tech, says Molly Russell’s father
data.description:
Meta’s defeat in a landmark social media trial must be the turning point for big tech, forcing them to curb addictive features, Molly Russell’s father has said.

Iain Russell, whose daughter Molly took her own life after viewing harmful images on Instagram, said the judgment should be a “watershed moment” for US tech giants to start taking responsibility for their effect on users’ lives.

He told The Telegraph: “The last 24 hours, I hope, we will be able to look back on as a pivot point for meaningful change in preventing addiction and harm to young people.”

A jury in Los Angeles on Wednesday found Instagram-owner Meta and YouTube-owner Google liable for a 20-year-old woman’s social media addiction.

The tech giants were ordered to pay $6m (£4.5m) in damages to the woman over claims that their apps “rewired” how children “think, feel and behave”.

Mr Russell’s daughter died after viewing more than 2,000 images of depression, self-harm and suicide on social media, a coroner found in 2022.

He said: “We had wished the end of Molly’s inquest to be a watershed moment. But so little has changed in the eight years.

“The case in California has unequivocally reopened that debate in terms of the addictive design of the platforms.”

Mr Russell said Sir Keir Starmer should now force firms to restrict addictive features on apps.

“We know the platforms are not going to do that by themselves. It will come down to regulation to make that happen. For it to be a watershed moment that saves lives, Sir Keir Starmer needs to do something,” he said.

The UK has been exploring limits, including barring under-16s from social media apps or digital curfews. Officials have been considering whether addictive features that encourage “doom-scrolling” should be barred for children.

The value of Mark Zuckerberg’s tech empire fell by $100bn on Thursday as investors prepare for potentially billions of dollars in further litigation following the judgment.

In the US trial, a California jury found in favour of the woman who said she had started using YouTube aged six and Instagram aged nine.

She claimed the negligence of the tech giants was to blame for her addiction to social media, which led to mental health problems including social phobia, body dysmorphic disorder and self-harm.

‘Victory is a great day for kids’

Mr Zuckerberg’s company faces a series of legal battles, likely to last years, against families, schools and US states.

Meta is now bracing for tens of billions of dollars in potential damages after its defeat in a landmark social media addiction trial.

The social media giant, which also owns Facebook, warned investors in a filing last year that it had exposure in certain cases in the “high tens of billions of dollars” from a series of social media lawsuits.

A Meta spokesman said: “We respectfully disagree with the verdict and will appeal. Teen mental health is profoundly complex and cannot be linked to a single app. 

“We will continue to defend ourselves vigorously, as every case is different, and we remain confident in our record of protecting teens online.”

The jury’s verdict came just a day after a separate lawsuit in New Mexico ordered Meta to pay $375m for failures in protecting children from child sexual abuse.

Dan Ives, a technology analyst, said a “tidal wave of lawsuits” was coming for Meta and Google. He predicted the ultimate damages to Meta could be “in the billions”.

In the LA case, Meta had argued there were other causes for her mental health challenges. Adam Mosseri, the head of Instagram, also gave evidence arguing that heavy social media use was different from being “clinically addicted”.

Arturo Bejar, a former Meta employee and whistleblower, said: “Victory is a great day for kids.”

He added: “This bellwether verdict opens the door for other families, as well as other states, to follow suit.”

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Next boss warns Government against profiteering from energy crisis
data.description:
The boss of Next has warned the Government against “profiting” from the Iran crisis as soaring oil and gas prices are expected to trigger higher tax revenues.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise said it was a “reasonable ask” for the Treasury not to retain any unexpected profits from disruption in the Middle East at a time when businesses are grappling with inflated costs.

His comments mark an escalation in tensions between the Government and the private sector after Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, blamed businesses for higher prices in the wake of the war.

The chief executive of Next said: “I think a reasonable ask from our industry – and in fact all industry – is that the Government doesn’t end up profiting from it.”

He singled out the prospect of higher revenues from fuel duty owing to increased petrol and diesel prices.

Lord Wolfson said it would be fair for the Treasury to take “what it was expecting from fuel but not necessarily more as a result of the price changes”.

“I think that would be a very reasonable ask for the industry to say that to the Government: ‘Don’t actually make more money out of this than you were expecting,’” he said.

Petrol forecourt bosses have hit back at claims they were profiteering from higher prices, arguing instead that the Treasury takes a bigger share of fuel sales than they did.

According to figures from the RAC, the retailer margin on a litre of petrol currently stands at 6pc.

Meanwhile, fuel duty comes in at 52.95p per litre. On top of that is VAT, charged at 20pc.

Petrol prices have risen almost 17p per litre since the start of the Iran conflict while diesel prices have risen around 33p per litre.

The Government is facing mounting pressure to help bring down prices for motorists.

Robert Jenrick, Reform’s economic spokesman, called for an emergency halving of VAT on road fuel on Thursday. He accused the Chancellor of “raking it in while motorists suffer”.

The Conservatives have urged the Government to scrap the upcoming fuel duty increase.

Previous analysis has suggested that the Treasury could also bank as much as £3bn more in tax revenues if the war in the Middle East results in higher oil and gas prices for longer.

Granville Park Partners said last week that it expected soaring energy costs to increase the corporation tax paid by North Sea firms by £1.5bn alone, while the windfall tax on energy companies’ profits would boost the Treasury’s coffers by a further £1.3bn if current oil and gas prices are sustained.

While rising prices at the pump won’t increase the fixed pence amount of fuel duty HMRC collects with every litre sold, the amount owed in VAT will.

While levied at 20pc, in line with most other goods and services, crucially, it is applied after fuel duty, constituting what many have called a “tax on a tax”. Higher petrol and diesel prices could deliver a windfall of £2.5bn to the Treasury.

Lord Wolfson said businesses should not be asking for tax cuts or reductions in levies to help them deal with extra costs, saying this would be “problematic”.

He weighed in on the debate after Next unveiled a £15m hit from the Middle East conflict, which could force it to push up prices as early as this summer.

The fashion retailer said the war in Iran was likely to have “knock-on effects” across its business, as it battles higher air freight fees and inflated fuel costs.

If the disruption drags on longer than expected, Next said it would have to start increasing prices in its stores as soon as June. Further price increases would come in the autumn.

Lord Wolfson said these would be “no more than 10pc”. However, it said this was currently a “contingency, not a plan”.

In its latest trading update on Thursday, the company said: “Much will depend on how long the conflict persists, and how much permanent damage is done to the world’s energy infrastructure.”

Next said if the war continued, extra costs would be reflected in “higher prices to consumers and disruption to our supply chain, both of which are likely to suppress sales”.

There are mounting fears that the war in Iran will start piling fresh pressure on shoppers as global trade chaos and higher energy costs threaten price rises.

Goldman Sachs has warned that inflation in Britain could hit 5pc this year “in a severely adverse scenario”. This compares to inflation of around 3pc last month, with households facing higher energy bills and grocery costs.

The Institute of Grocery Distribution has said food inflation could hit 8pc by June 2026 if the oil shock persisted.

However, Next’s warning suggests shoppers should brace for price rises not just in supermarkets, but across the high street.

Air cargo prices have already soared following strikes across the Middle East, as companies battle to avoid turmoil in key shipping lanes through the region. Rates rose 10pc this week, according to WorldACD Market Data, following an 8pc increase a week earlier. It also reflects sharp increases in fuel costs since the outbreak of war in Iran.

The price of Brent crude is up by more than a third since the conflict began, as Tehran halts oil flows out of the Gulf. Oil prices rose 2.5pc to nearly $105 in early trading on Thursday.

Next issued its sales warning as it said profits jumped 14.5pc to £1.2bn in the year to January. This was ahead of its earlier guidance, driven by better sales in January.

The retailer said sales had been “encouraging in the UK” since the end of its financial year, and had also been “strong overseas up to the point the conflict began in the Middle East”.

However, it said: “We have not yet reached the period of unusually strong UK trading we experienced last year and, perhaps more importantly, instability in the Middle East – which represents around 6pc of our total turnover – may continue to restrain growth in that region.”

data.title: NS&I victims to be hit with IHT bills
data.description:
Victims of the NS&I savings scandal face being hit with inheritance tax bills after they are reunited with missing money that is rightfully theirs.

Torsten Bell, the pensions minister, said on Thursday that up to 37,500 beneficiaries of deceased savers who had accounts at National Savings and Investments (NS&I) had been denied £476m because of operational failures.

It was also announced that Dax Harkins, the former chief executive of NS&I, was forced to resign over the scandal. His position became untenable after The Telegraph revealed a series of failings at the bank.

They included accusations that NS&I had short-changed bereaved families by losing track of dead customers’ investments, and withheld premium bonds, which are entered in a monthly draw with prizes up to £1m.

In a statement in the Commons, Mr Bell said victims of the scandal would be compensated. While he claimed taxpayers would not pay the bill, he suggested families could be hit with tax bills after they were given funds they had previously been denied.

This is because the sums could alter the value of deceased savers’ estates for tax purposes, raising the prospect of additional charges. While technically the estates would be taxed, it would affect those who are being reunited with the money.

Mr Bell said: “We also recognise there may be tax implications for affected estates and want to avoid bereaved families facing disproportionate disruption and administrative costs as a result of this error.

“We are exploring what support can be provided and will set this out alongside the NS&I delivery plan in May.”

Compensation payouts for the infected blood scandal and the Post Office Horizon scandal were free of tax.

Mr Bell announced that Sir Jim Harra, the former head of HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC), had been drafted in to replace Mr Harkins.

He will report on a delivery plan in May, detailing exactly how many people have been affected; how their savings will be returned with interest, and what additional compensation will be paid.

Mr Harkins was appointed chief executive of NS&I in April 2023, having worked his way up the organisation from the sales department.

Modernisation a ‘full-spectrum disaster’

He took home £300,000 in salary and pension perks in 2025, although it is understood he was denied a bonus for failing to keep a lid on a spiralling bill to modernise NS&I – a project condemned as a “full-spectrum disaster” in a report in February.

The Telegraph discovered that some families had been forced to hire lawyers to obtain cash that was rightfully theirs because the savings bank could not find customers’ accounts.

In one case, NS&I was forced to refund a customer’s family for tax interest and legal costs after the savings bank lost track of two accounts linked to an investment portfolio.

In another case, NS&I failed to tell the daughter of a saver who had died about several bonds her mother had owned, and appeared to lose track of another £2,000 in Premium Bonds the daughter owned.

One widower was denied prizes paid to his late wife’s accounts following a failure of NS&I’s phone lines that meant a “straightforward process” turned into “a prolonged and unhappy experience”.

Addressing MPs, Mr Bell said NS&I had failed to learn from past missing savings scandals.

Comparing the scandal to Santander’s “serious failings” in 2018 in processing the accounts and investments of deceased customers, he told MPs: “What is now clear is that NS&I did not respond to those warning signs as fully as I, and more importantly their customers, would expect, nor did the last government act.”

Mr Bell said his three priorities since learning of the scandal had been to act immediately; to reunite beneficiaries of dead customers with funds held by NS&I, and to achieve institutional change at the savings bank.

The pensions minister insisted NS&I had received written assurances from its current suppliers that the issue would not affect any more customers. Atos, the previous supplier for bereavement cases until 2025, had pledged its full co-operation with inquiries.

He added that the Government would act to “reunite beneficiaries with any funds that NS&I holds”. However, there is now a question over who funds the compensation.

It is understood either NS&I or the Treasury will pay, meaning taxpayers are still on the hook.

‘Government must ensure every penny is repaid’

Sir Mel Stride, the shadow chancellor, said: “This is a shocking failure at the heart of a state-backed bank with tens of thousands of savers denied money that is rightfully theirs.

“Heads have rightly rolled, but serious questions remain about how this was allowed to happen and why it took so long to act. The Government must ensure every penny is repaid swiftly and guarantee taxpayers are not left footing the bill for yet another public sector fiasco.”

Andrew Griffith, the shadow business secretary, said: “This failure raises questions about the Treasury’s own supervision including what they have done since being informed on Dec 18, 2025 and The Telegraph breaking this story this week.

“There must be no rewards for failure so ministers should be using ‘claw back’ to recoup past payments to the CEO and management. This is an epic failure to deliver what should be a simple suite of savings products, which many private sector providers would have delivered in months at a fraction of the price.”

Launched in 1861, NS&I was originally known as the Post Office Savings Bank. It was established to provide a secure place for ordinary citizens to save while funding government spending.

It introduced Premium Bonds in 1956 and became an independent government department in 1969, rebranding to NS&I in 2002. The bank, which looks after £240bn for some 24 million customers, has apologised for the failings.

data.title: New BBC boss under pressure to reveal Google shares
data.description:
The incoming director-general of the BBC is under pressure to reveal how many shares he owns in Google amid concerns his financial stake in the tech giant could present a conflict of interest.

Matt Brittin, a former senior Google executive who was confirmed as the successor to Tim Davie on Wednesday, is in discussions with the BBC about his personal interests before taking up the top job in May.

The BBC has so far refused to confirm how many shares Mr Brittin still holds in Google’s parent company, Alphabet, where he was an executive for almost two decades.

Shares often comprise a significant portion of overall pay for senior Google executives. Sundar Pichai, Google’s chief executive, is paid a base salary of $2m (£1.5m) but stands to earn as much as $692m in the next three years thanks to stock awards.

While pay for top US executives must be disclosed publicly, the same does not apply for those employed by a UK subsidiary.

The tech boss, who led Google in Europe, the Middle East and Africa, before stepping down last year, refused to tell MPs in 2016 how much money he was paid by the business.

His potential stake in the tech giant has triggered concerns about a potential conflict of interest as the BBC directly competes with Google-owned YouTube for audiences, especially when it comes to younger viewers.

Dame Chi Onwurah, chairman of the Commons science, innovation and technology select committee, said that Mr Brittin’s potential shareholding in Google raises conflict of interest concerns.

“In my view, the BBC and Google are competitors and if Mr Brittin has an interest in Google’s success, there are undoubtedly areas where that is not the same thing as the BBC’s success and maybe even runs counter to it,” she said.

She added: “At a time of increasing public concern about accountability, particularly from big tech and the media, I think it’s critical that the BBC should be transparent about the recruitment process and the interests of its director-general.”

The BBC said it would discuss mitigations for any conflict in Mr Brittin’s personal interests, adding that a declaration of his interests will be published on May 18, his first day.

It is thought that these mitigations could include placing his shares in a blind trust or selling them off.

One senior shadow minister added: “It’s critical that anyone in such a sensitive position is beyond reproach and that of course means selling any Google shares or investments which would compromise his position.”

A BBC source said: “Clearly Matt will do whatever is required to ensure there is no conflict of interest when he assumes the role of director general.”

Fears of big tech

The BBC has been attempting to strengthen its ties with YouTube as the public service broadcaster seeks to stem an exodus of viewers to the US tech platform.

The corporation struck a landmark deal in January to make programmes specifically for YouTube, rather than using the service simply to promote clips and trailers of its shows.

In its annual plan published on Thursday, the BBC reiterated plans to “scale our offer on YouTube to help connect with our lightest audiences in new ways”.

But the BBC is also at loggerheads with Google as the tech giant rolls out AI features in its search engine.

In a recent submission to regulators, the BBC warned that more action was needed to ensure publishers were given “meaningful” control over how the tech giant uses their content. A spokesman for the BBC said the broadcaster stood by its submission.

The BBC also has a relationship with Google through the tech giant’s advertising platform. The broadcaster currently only makes advertising revenue from its operations overseas but adverts could be introduced in the UK under licence fee alternatives being considered as part of the charter renewal.

Mr Brittin, a Cambridge University graduate and Doctor Who fan, has pledged to win back the public’s trust after a string of scandals engulfing the BBC, not least the doctoring of a Donald Trump speech in a Panorama episode that led to Mr Davie’s resignation.

However, his lack of journalistic experience and ties to big tech have triggered concerns in some quarters. John Sergeant, the veteran BBC journalist, this week described Mr Brittin’s appointment as “disastrous” because he “doesn’t know about journalism”.

Baroness Kidron, the former film director and crossbench peer, said the appointment was “another decision from government that treats big tech as entirely neutral when clearly it is hell bent on absorbing all UK assets”.

She added: “I have no problem with Matt as an individual. He is clearly a competent leader and whenever I met him perfectly charming but there is a view of the world he had presided over in which Google fought against paying tax in the UK, has stolen UK creative copyright, including the BBC’s, and has been careless with children’s wellbeing and safety.

“The challenge for Government is to prove this is not part of their plans to give away the UK creative sector to big tech by backing transparency and copyright.”

A BBC spokesman said: “Matt will comply with BBC policy in the same way every other BBC employee does.”

Mr Brittin has been contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Some of our apprentices can’t tell the time, HR boss admits
data.description:
Some apprentices at water company Severn Trent struggle to tell the time, an executive at the company has said.

Neil Morrison, the HR director at Severn Trent in the Midlands, said around 10pc of young people joining its apprenticeship programme had the numeracy skills of an 11-year-old.

“Things like telling the time are problematic,” he told a House of Lords committee.

The admission prompted gasps from those in attendance, leading Mr Morrison to say that it was “a very bleak picture”.

Children are taught to tell the time from around six to seven years old in the British state school system. By 11, they are expected to not only tell the time on analogue and digital clocks but also know their times tables.

A spokesman for Severn Trent said some struggled to recognise that 15:00 is 3pm on the twenty-four-hour clock, for instance.

Struggles to tell the time and do basic maths can create “a problem in terms of shift patterns, doing basic calculations and struggling with percentages or deductions of things like payslips, tax and National Insurance”, Mr Morrison told Lords.

Severn Trent, one of Britain’s major water companies, hires around 100 apprentices each year. It offers apprentices training on maths skills up to a GCSE level.

Given the relatively small size of the intake, Mr Morrison said it was likely that there were a large number of people across Britain with similarly poor numeracy skills who may also struggle to tell the time and were going without support.

“The stat for the Midlands is that 53pc of people come out with less than a grade four in maths,” he said. It means more than half of the population was only taught maths up to the level of a child aged 10 or 11.

Mr Morrison was giving evidence as part of the “Numeracy for Life” committee’s inquiry into maths skills in Britain.

Poor maths skills in the general population present a problem for Severn Trent, Mr Morrison said, as customers struggle to understand the numbers in their bills. The company tries to overcome the problem by using visuals and charts, he added.

Col Alexander Burt, who convened the committee hearing, called the testimony “very bleak” and asked: “Are things getting better or worse – can you give us some hope?”

Severn Trent’s Mr Morrison said, for the most disadvantaged in society, the situation was getting worse.

“Unfortunately the gap they experience is getting worse, so it becomes more important to focus on those people,” he said.

The Government is fighting to understand and overcome the problems caused by low levels of numeracy across Britain.

The Department for Education recently rolled out a core skills diploma for pupils who fail English and maths at GCSE in order to help the disproportionately white working-class cohort gain qualifications that will ultimately help them enter the workforce.

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
