data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: US court blocks Pentagon’s attempt to ‘cripple’ blacklisted AI company
data.description:
A US court has blocked the Trump administration’s attempts to ban the artificial intelligence company Anthropic.

In a defeat for the Pentagon, a judge has rejected a proposal by Pete Hegseth, US secretary of war, to declare the company a supply chain risk for refusing to allow unrestricted use of its technology for military purposes.

The court said the move could “cripple” Anthropic, which is worth $380bn (£285bn) and is one of America’s biggest AI companies.

Anthropic’s Claude bot is already in use by the Pentagon. In recent months, its technology and data analysis were used for the capture of Nicolas Maduro and to identify strike targets in Donald Trump’s war on Iran.

But the sides have clashed over Anthropic’s refusal to allow the Pentagon to use its technology for autonomous weapons and surveillance of Americans.

Mr Trump has called them a “radical left, woke company”, and Mr Hegseth said last month that he was branding the company a supply chain risk, a label usually reserved for suppliers to hostile military powers.

Anthropic sued the Trump administration to overturn this, and a California judge granted an injunction on Thursday night, pending a full legal case.

“Defendants’ designation of Anthropic as a ‘supply chain risk’ is likely both contrary to law and arbitrary and capricious,” the judge wrote.

“These broad measures do not appear to be directed at the government’s stated national security interests. Instead, these measures appear designed to punish Anthropic.

“The evidence shows that they would cripple Anthropic.

“Nothing in the governing statute supports the Orwellian notion that an American company may be branded a potential adversary and saboteur of the US for expressing disagreement with the government.”

The supply chain risk designation would have prevented US federal agencies from working with Anthropic, even for non-military use. It would also have prevented military suppliers from using it when performing work for the Pentagon.

On a strict reading, it would have required companies such as Google and Amazon, which have both invested in Anthropic, to cut ties with the company.

Anthropic had warned that the move would have cost it hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue.

The company thanked the court for a “swift decision” and said: “Our focus remains on working productively with the government to ensure all Americans benefit from safe, reliable AI.”

Emil Michael, the US under secretary of war, who had been leading negotiations with Anthropic, called the ruling a “disgrace”.

“There are dozens of factual errors in the 42-page judgment rushed out in 48 hours DURING A TIME OF CONFLICT that seeks to upend the [president’s] role as commander in chief and disrupt [the secretary of war’s] full ability to conduct military operations with the partners it chooses,” he said on social media.

The US government has a week to appeal.

data.title: Iran energy crisis ‘worse than 1970s and Ukraine war combined’
data.description:
The world is facing a worse energy shock than the twin crises of the 1970s and the turmoil that followed Russia’s invasion of Ukraine combined, the boss of the International Energy Agency (IEA) has warned.

Fatih Birol, the executive director of the IEA, said no country would be immune from the impact of the war on energy prices. He described the conflict in the Middle East as “very severe”.

Mr Birol said the IEA was consulting with governments in Europe and Asia on whether to release more of the world’s stockpiles of oil as the conflict entered its fourth week.

At least 40 energy assets had been “severely or very severely” damaged in the Middle East owing to the war, he added.

Donald Trump said over the weekend that he would “obliterate” Iran’s power plants if it did not fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the vital trade route, within 48 hours. The deadline is set to expire at close to midnight on Monday.

Iran responded to the US president by warning it would “irreversibly destroy” energy sites across the Middle East if he followed through on his threat.

Speaking in Australia late on Sunday night, Mr Birol said: “This crisis as things stand is now two oil crises and one gas crash put all together.

“The global economy is facing a major, major threat today and I very much hope that this issue will be resolved as soon as possible.

“No country will be immune to the effects of this crisis if it continues to go in this direction. So there is a need for global efforts.”

Mr Birol said the “most important solution” to the war was reopening the Strait of Hormuz, the waterway along Iran’s coastline where 20pc of global oil and gas exports pass.

The IEA said traffic through the strait had slowed to “a trickle” following attacks on vessels and energy infrastructure in the Middle East.

Mr Birol appeared to take a swipe at Mr Trump as the conflict showed no sign of ending.

“The ​depth of the problem was not well appreciated by the decision-makers around the world,” he said.

The price of oil was trading at around $113 a barrel on Monday morning and Mr Birol said there would not be a specific crude price that would trigger another release of global stockpiles.

IEA member nations agreed earlier this month to release a record 400 million barrels of oil from strategic stockpiles to ‌combat the spike in ‌global crude prices. ⁠The drawdown represented 20pc of overall stocks.

“A stock release will help to comfort the markets but this is not the solution,” said Mr Birol.

“It will only help to ⁠reduce the pain in the economy.”

He has begun a world tour in Canberra, saying the Asia-Pacific is at the forefront of the oil crisis given its reliance on the Strait of Hormuz for imports of oil and other crucial products such as fertiliser and helium.

After meeting Anthony Albanese, the Australian prime minister, Mr Birol will travel to ‌Japan later this week before a G7 meeting.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Lidl to sell plug-in solar panels under Miliband net zero drive
data.description:
Plug-in solar panels will go on sale in budget supermarket Lidl as part of Ed Miliband’s net zero drive.

The devices will cost as little as £400 upfront and could cut a typical household’s power bill by £70-£110 a year, the Government said.

They can be hung from a balcony, placed in the garden or elsewhere and then plugged into an ordinary wall socket, without the need for any home modifications.

Under current regulations, they cannot be sold. But plug-in solar is already widespread in other European countries, and ministers have vowed to change the rules in Britain within months.

The Government has held talks with Lidl about launching the products and is also speaking with Amazon and the manufacturer, EcoFlow, “to enable them to be brought to the UK market”.

It comes amid fears that the Middle East conflict will drive up household energy bills by hundreds of pounds if the crisis does not abate soon.

Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, has vowed to “double down” on the rollout of solar and wind farms in response, including by making plug-in solar panels available.

Shoppers may soon be able to pick up the portable power plants in Lidl’s much-loved “middle aisle” where the chain sells a variety of homeware, clothing and appliances.

In Germany, Lidl already sells small balcony solar panel “starter sets” for as little as €149 (£129). They are capable of generating up to 400 watts of electricity, enough to cater for a significant chunk of a typical household’s daily needs.

‘Win for consumers’

Mr Miliband said: “The Iran war has once again shown our drive for clean power is essential for our energy security, so we can escape the grip of fossil fuel markets we don’t control.

“Whether through solar panels fitted as standard on new homes or making it possible for people to purchase plug-in solar in shops, we are determined to roll out clean power so we can give our country energy sovereignty.”

Georgina Hall, an executive at Lidl, said: “We are committed to making sustainable living affordable for everyone, and we welcome the Government’s move to modernise regulations in the UK.

“Updating the regulatory landscape for this ‘plug-and-play’ technology is a positive step towards empowering British households to manage their energy costs and support the nation’s net-zero ambitions.”

Challenger energy supplier Fuse is preparing to launch this year its own plug-in solar panel with a capacity of more than two kilowatts, equivalent to 2,000 watts.

It will cost between £1,000 and £2,000 and comes with a battery, allowing households, including renters, to save up to £500 a year, the company claims.

Alan Chang, Fuse’s chief executive, said: “The UK’s move to legalise plug-in solar is a huge win for consumers.”

Additional initiative

In another move aimed at cutting bills, the Government also confirmed it will launch a trial scheme that will allow some households to save on their energy bills when excess wind power is generated.

The scheme, which is mainly expected to benefit people in Scotland and eastern England, will allow energy suppliers to offer customers cheap electricity during certain hours to encourage greater consumption.

It is designed to reduce the need for so-called curtailment, where wind farms are paid to switch off because the grid is too congested to accept their power. Curtailment – which is ultimately paid for by consumers through their bills – cost Britain a record £1.5bn last year.

By encouraging households to use extra power during these periods instead, ministers hope to cut the bill for switching off turbines.

It is understood that households will be exempt from paying levies on their electricity when “turning up” their consumption.

data.title: Can Matt Brittin rescue the BBC?
data.description:
The post of BBC director-general has become something of a poisoned chalice over the years. The incumbent is rarely free from controversy largely because the corporation is, uniquely, funded from a licence fee and backed by a Royal Charter. Several directors-general have been forced to resign over shortcomings in the BBC’s journalism, including the most recent, Tim Davie. He stepped down after a row over the editing by Panorama of a speech by Donald Trump and the alleged failure of the management to act on complaints about impartiality made by its own editorial standards committee.

The BBC board is now thought to have chosen a successor in Matt Brittin, a former senior executive with Google. He is unquestionably a high achiever as a Cambridge rowing blue and Olympic competitor. At Google he became the company’s president for Europe, the Middle East and Africa, before leaving last year. But does any of this qualify him to run the BBC as its editor-in-chief? He has no relevant broadcasting experience, though given the fate of his predecessors that may be no bad thing. However, one of the big worries with the BBC is its perceived Left-of-centre culture.

As a former non-executive director of the Guardian Media Group and director of strategy at The Mirror, Mr Brittin would appear to fit that particular mindset even if it is his experience with Google that is being emphasised. As Google owns YouTube there is a certain rationale behind appointing someone from a digital milieu that is now a constant challenge to the whole idea of a public service broadcaster. BBC News now pursues a “digital-first” approach, moving away from a fixed timetable of bulletins and news shows.

But is it his task to chase younger audiences, who have largely deserted the corporation, or to preside over its retreat from programming excellence and impartial journalism? Above all, the new director-general has to make the case for public funding in a world of streaming and subscription channels.

In this he would appear to be able to rely on the Labour Government for support. In a speech last week, Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, compared the BBC to the NHS and questioned whether there should even be a renewable charter to underpin its existence. She said the Government’s policy was “about protecting the BBC – and everything that it represents – for the long term for all of us.” But for all this bluster, the biggest question remains unanswered: if the BBC is to continue to be funded by a licence fee, can Mr Brittin justify its retention in a digital era that he knows better than most?

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Britain risks missing out on the great coal revival
data.description:
Coal, the dirtiest of the fossil fuels, has long been considered the bogeyman of the energy world. But the Iran war looks set to change all that.

The three-week-old conflict has damaged Qatar’s main gas plant and all but closed the Strait of Hormuz, locking up a fifth of the world’s gas supply and sending prices spiralling.

In response, countries with a heavy reliance on gas are reviving underused coal-fired power plants. To feed the generators, they are expanding mining or snapping up supplies from big producers such as Australia and Indonesia.

Coal, it seems, is back from the dead.

“In the Indo-Pacific region, but also in Europe for that matter, any utility or government that can switch from natural gas to coal-fired power generation will be doing that at the moment,” says Henning Gloystein of the Eurasia Group.

The Iran war may be over in weeks, as Donald Trump predicts, or it may drag on. But with Qatar saying that a full resumption of gas production could be years away, and the Strait of Hormuz revealed as an unreliable conduit, the shift to coal could outlast the current turmoil.

“I wouldn’t be surprised if coal has more of a renaissance in the following year, potentially longer,” says Caleb Jasso, of the US-based Institute for Energy Research.

The coal resurgence will be greatest in countries that can produce it domestically, such as China and India. But major Asian importers such as South Korea, Taiwan and Japan are switching from gas to coal.

Even in green-focused Europe, countries that still have coal capacity like Germany and Poland will follow suit.

There is no room on the bandwagon for Britain, though, which has gone further than most in shutting down coal-fired power.

The last coal-fired power plants closed in 2024, and the only debate is about whether to exploit the remaining oil and gas reserves in the North Sea.

“Countries like China, Japan, South Korea and Vietnam never switched coal off. They maintained multiple baseloads, so when gas tightens, coal capacity can step in,” says Michelle Manook, head of the global industry body FutureCoal.

She says European countries like Britain, which have pushed coal out, can now only toggle between gas and renewables. 

“If there is a lesson, it is this: don’t build your energy system on a single pathway, or you risk exposing it when it matters most,” Manook says. 

For many countries, gas was supposed to be the transition fuel that bridged the gap between their coal-dependent past and their renewables-based future. Gas emits less carbon dioxide and pollutants such as sulphur dioxide than coal, and is more efficient.

Demand for gas from Asia has doubled in the past two decades. Coal consumption has also grown, but has since tailed off as investment in gas and green energy has increased.

But almost no country yet has enough renewable energy, such as solar or wind, to power its economy nor the storage capacity to make renewables a reliable replacement for fossil fuels.

So for many countries – particularly those without a nuclear industry – a gas crisis can only push countries one way: back towards coal.

Iran ripped the gas market apart when it shut the Strait of Hormuz, and then used a bombing raid to cull almost one-fifth of Qatar’s production capacity for at least the next three years.

Even if the war blows over soon, Qatar’s damage will leave the gas market tighter than before. And the Asian countries that rely on Qatar can no longer trust the Strait of Hormuz.

Expectations of tight supply and a worldwide scramble for gas have doubled the price of liquefied natural gas (LNG) in Asia, taking it to a three-year high.

The response has been immediate: coal is back on the agenda.

Bangladesh, the Philippines, Thailand and Vietnam are among the countries looking to step up coal imports and boost coal-fired power generation.

Indonesia, a major coal exporter, has backtracked on plans to cut coal output this year and will now boost production. The government has also said, though, that it will prioritise domestic users over exports.

The economic powerhouses of Japan, Taiwan and South Korea, which still produce about a third of their energy from coal, are also following suit.

“We are seeing South Korea eliminating some of their coal capacity caps. We are seeing Taiwan look at restarting a mothballed coal power plant. We’re seeing strategic buying of coal stockpiles. They’re recognising the risk, and they’re hedging right now,” says Anthony Knutson, of analyst firm Wood Mackenzie.

Countries that make a lot of their own coal, such as China, India and Pakistan, are looking to boost output.

“In India and China, 85pc to 90pc of the coal is typically supplied domestically. So they are pivoting right now to incentivise more domestic coal production,” Knutson says.

Countries in Europe that still have the capacity to generate power from coal, such as Germany and Poland, will also feel tempted to pull this lever.

‘Coal has a bad rap’

But the European Union’s net zero targets create a strong political headwind.

Poland has cut coal’s share of electricity generation from 80pc in 2018 to 53pc last year, but the soaring cost of gas may stall this momentum.

Gloystein says the Poles will want to emulate China, where a transition to nuclear and green energy is backed up by coal as a safety valve.

“The calls to burn coal for longer will be very loud, because they have a big lignite industry that employs a lot of people and has strong unions,” he says.

In Germany, Jasso says the need for coal is more acute, given the reliance on imported gas and the lack of nuclear energy, but the political resistance is stronger.

“The biggest obstacle to getting back into coal is the rhetoric that has become so deeply set against it. Coal has a bad rap,” he says.

“I am fearful to see what pain point Europeans are going to have to endure in order to get to the real conversation of how do we become more energy self-reliant and diversify our imports.”

Gloystein reckons there is a majority in the German parliament to back a law that has already been drafted to bring back into service the 6.5 gigawatts of reserve coal capacity.

Yet if the country struggles to refill its gas reserves over the summer, the government may decide to bring the draft law to a vote, he says.

“And if gas prices remain high for another five or six years, the Germans might even start fiddling with their 2038 coal phase-out,” Gloystein adds. 

As for Britain, there is nothing left to switch on.

Manook says reembracing coal doesn’t have to be at odds with emissions-reduction targets if coal is made more efficient and its emissions are captured and stored.

“Across Asia, the focus has been on abating coal – improving efficiency and reducing emissions through advanced technologies,” she says.

But there are other drawbacks to coal. Its price tends to rise when gas prices rise and has jumped since the start of the Iran war.

Supply is unlikely to be directly disrupted by war, but major coal producers are regularly hit by cyclones, floods or earthquakes. There is also an ever-diminishing supply pipeline, as so few new mines are being commissioned.

The Iran war will also hit the cost of coal by pushing up the prices miners pay for diesel and for shipping their product.

“If you can’t produce coal domestically, you’re basically replacing one import risk with the other,” Gloystein says.

Kieran Tompkins, of Capital Economics, acknowledges that coal can increase diversity of supply for importing countries and self-reliance for producers. 

But its resurgence is really part of a bigger question: just how do they inoculate themselves against a chaotic and conflict-prone world?

“The legacy of the current crisis could be that energy security becomes an increasing priority,” he says.

data.title: Britain heading for recession, Morgan Stanley warns
data.description:
The UK is heading for a recession if the energy price shock continues, according to a Wall Street bank.

Morgan Stanley said the Bank of England could raise interest rates if energy prices fail to come down, which would be likely to cause an economic downturn by the end of 2026.

Bruna Skarica, chief UK economist at Morgan Stanley, said Britain would face “a pronounced UK recession at the turn of the year” if energy prices stay at recent highs in the coming months and borrowing costs rise.

The war in Iran has caused oil and gas prices to soar in recent weeks, sparking fears over inflationary pressures and the impact on the UK economy.

The fallout from the Middle East conflict led to the Institute of Grocery Distribution warning on Wednesday that food inflation could hit 8pc by June 2026 if the oil shock persists. 

That is more than double the current rate of 3.6pc.

Huw Pill, the chief economist at the Bank of England, said on Tuesday that “there are limits to what the MPC [Monetary Policy Committee] can do” to protect households from the energy price shock.

However, he stressed that Threadneedle Street “stands ready” to raise interest rates if the conflict in the Middle East drags on.

“The fog of uncertainty in which we always operate ​cannot be an excuse for inaction,” he said in a speech on Tuesday.

Mr Pill’s intervention comes as a growing number of economists warn that the energy crisis threatens to push the UK into a downturn.

Simon French, the chief economist at City stockbroker Panmure Liberum, said a recession in the final six months of the year was now “a real possibility”.

He added: “Events in the Middle East can, of course, reverse, but the UK economy’s soft underbelly has been revealed. It cannot be unseen.”

Thomas Pugh, the chief economist at RSM UK, a tax and consulting firm, said: “Everything depends on how energy prices move going forward, but we now expect growth of around 0.5pc this year with a decent chance of a recession.”

The war in Iran triggered the sharpest rise in factory costs since Black Wednesday in 1992, when Britain withdrew the pound from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism, triggering a huge sell-off of sterling that led to soaring interest rates.

The jump in factory input prices recorded by S&P Global in March underlines the effect of the Middle East conflict on manufacturers, which are heavily reliant on fuel and energy-intensive materials.

Both oil and gas prices have surged since the war broke out late last month, as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has strangled fuel supplies.

Echoes of Black Wednesday

Chris Williamson, of S&P Global, said: “Inflationary pressures have surged higher on the back of rising energy prices and fractured supply chains.

“The acceleration in cost growth in the manufacturing sector was especially severe, being the sharpest since the depreciation of sterling following Black Wednesday.”

The comparison with Black Wednesday will worry investors. At the time, then-chancellor Norman Lamont withdrew the currency from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism after failing to maintain the value of the pound.

The move led to sterling plunging by 25pc against the US dollar and 15pc against the German mark, with market panic prompting the Bank of England to raise interest rates from 10pc to 15pc.

Growth stalls as inflation rises

Alongside a rapid increase in manufacturing costs, Tuesday’s closely watched S&P survey also revealed that private sector activity in Britain fell to a six-month low with a score of 51, just above the 50-point mark indicating no growth.

Mr Williamson said the conflict in the Middle East had “hit the UK economy in March, stalling growth while driving inflation sharply higher”.

He added: “The full impact on inflation and economic growth depends not just on the duration of the war but also on the length of disruptions to energy markets and shipping.”

Meanwhile, in the US, business output slumped to an 11-month low in March, according to the S&P Global flash US purchasing managers’ index (PMI).

Companies raised their sale prices at the fastest pace since August 2022 and began cutting staff as they grappled with supply-chain delays and higher energy costs.

Mr Williamson said the data indicated that consumer price inflation will surge to 4pc, up from the current 2.8pc.

He said: “Companies are reporting a hit to demand from the additional uncertainty and cost of living impact generated by the conflict.

“Travel, transport and tourism related issues are compounded by financial market jitters and affordability constraints, notably including concern over the impact of higher interest rates, surging energy prices and supply-chain delays.”

The war has sent US borrowing costs soaring, with both two-year and ten-year treasury yields rising at their sharpest rate since October 2024 this month. 

Separate figures on Tuesday revealed that, in Britain, business optimism has tumbled to its lowest level since last June.

Jake Finney, a senior economist at PwC, said: “Private sector growth slowed considerably in March as events in the Middle East clouded the economic outlook. Demand has weakened, cost pressures have picked up, and the pace of job cuts has accelerated.”

Around a quarter of manufacturers said they had experienced longer delivery times from suppliers, causing some companies to build up stock in case of supply chain disruption.

Alongside cost pressures, businesses have warned that poor economic conditions weighed on retail sales in March.

Figures from the Confederation of British Industry showed that retail sales volumes fell at their fastest pace since the pandemic for the month so far, as cost of living fears caused shoppers to rein in spending.

It marks the fastest decline since April 2020, when many stores were closed during the first pandemic lockdown.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Miliband’s clean energy tsar calls for more North Sea drilling
data.description:
The chairman of Ed Miliband’s state-backed energy company has argued for “more North Sea production” to slow job losses in the oil and gas sector.

Jürgen Maier, the former Siemens boss who now oversees Great British Energy (GB Energy), said more domestic oil and gas drilling was needed to buy time for workers in the North Sea so they could retrain for net zero jobs.

He said increased drilling would also generate higher tax revenues and produce fewer carbon emissions than imports.

Writing on LinkedIn, Mr Maier said that while he disagreed that higher North Sea production could cut energy prices, he supported the move to renewables “being an all energy and managed energy transition”.

The chairman’s comments will raise eyebrows at a time when critics of Labour’s energy policies say the Energy Secretary’s crackdown on the North Sea is accelerating the basin’s decline and causing thousands of job losses.

Within hours of his remarks being reported, Mr Maier clarified in another post that he was “fully supportive” of the Government’s position.

A spokesman for Mr Miliband declined to comment on whether any officials had contacted Mr Maier about his comments and referred The Telegraph to GB Energy.

A report by trade body Offshore Energy UK on Tuesday warned that “policy, not geology” was behind a collapse in investment.

It warned that on current trajectories, Britain will be reliant on liquefied natural gas (LNG) shipped from abroad for nearly half of its gas supplies by 2035.

By comparison, it argued, this could fall to less than 10pc if ministers reformed the windfall tax and granted new licences for dozens of projects.

The creation of GB Energy has been one of Mr Miliband’s flagship policies since returning to government in July 2024. Mr Maier was appointed as the company’s chairman that same month.

The debate around the North Sea has flared in recent weeks as the crisis in the Middle East has prompted global concern about oil and gas supplies and sent prices soaring.

On Tuesday, Mr Miliband repeated his insistence that drilling for more oil and gas in the North Sea would make “no difference” to production.

The Energy Secretary has repeatedly argued that more drilling would be a waste of time because it would not bring down prices.

In a parliamentary question, Esther McVey, a Conservative MP, said sites were being closed down because of the Energy Secretary’s “Left-wing dogma”.

But Mr Miliband said: “We’re going to use the existing oil and gas fields for their lifetime. I think she’s referring to the question of exploration licences.

“What everybody says is exploration licences make no material difference to production levels and on the tax question.

“I hope she’ll carry on supporting the windfall tax and tell her front bench this would be the wrong time to abolish the windfall tax.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: UK hits Chinese steel with 50pc tariffs to protect British industry
data.description:
Britain is preparing to hit foreign steel with tariffs of up to 50pc after warnings that a flood of cheap Chinese imports risked putting UK furnaces out of business.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed on Thursday that quotas for tariff-free imports will be cut by three fifths, with new duties placed on anything above this.

The protections echo similar crackdowns by the EU and the US, which have also moved to shield their domestic industries from cheap steel that is pouring in from China, Vietnam, Turkey and other countries with lower environmental standards.

Major producers, including Tata Steel UK, are understood to have warned that their businesses faced a potentially existential threat without urgent action.

Speaking to reporters in Port Talbot, South Wales – where Tata is building the world’s biggest electric arc furnace with government support – Mr Kyle said the combined moves would allow “the domestic market to flourish”.

He said: “This is what the sector is telling us that they need. This is the kind of investment that is aligned with where the steel sector itself, from across the breadth of the steel sector, is saying that it needs support.

“What you’re seeing today is a Government that matches the ambition that the steel sector has for itself.”

While the tariffs were welcomed by executives, the industry will be under pressure from the rising cost of natural gas, which is used by steel mills, and electricity as the Middle East crisis causes a price shock.

The new tariffs came as Mr Kyle unveiled a new national steel strategy. It sets an ambition for half of the steel used in the UK to be made in Britain.

The strategy says steel production is a vital capability needed for defence and critical infrastructure. Ministers intervened last year to take control of the last remaining blast furnaces operated by British Steel amid fears that the company’s Chinese owner, Jingye, was about to shut them down.

However, the Business Secretary stopped short of guaranteeing that government projects such as the Lower Thames Crossing would meet the threshold of using 50pc British steel.

Mr Kyle said the sector had been viewed as in decline for the last 50 years, adding: “I’m not here to either manage or mitigate de-industrialisation. I am here to re-industrialise. That is the purpose.

“Steel is going to be essential as we invest in our defence going forward, as we invest in our infrastructure and renew our infrastructure for a new generation, whether that be for trains, whether that be building out for the Oxford-Cambridge Arc and some of the new runways that we’ll be building, so there’ll be huge demand for steel on that quantity.”

The Government’s measures to protect domestic steelmakers replace older so-called safeguarding measures that executives had warned were out of date and were too generous to foreign producers.

Steel imports to the UK meet around 70pc of demand, up from 55pc in 2022. The biggest increases have come from China, India, Vietnam, South Korea, Turkey and Algeria, according to trade body UK Steel.

The global steel market is experiencing a glut because of overproduction by China and other Asian countries.

According to the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), world capacity for steelmaking is now about 2.55 billion metric tonnes per year. That is about 680 million metric tonnes more than there is demand for.

British producers have also been hurt by rising energy costs, although various government schemes to subsidise their power costs are now in place and are expected to be expanded.

Tata Steel UK’s new electric arc furnace will benefit from this support when it begins operation in late 2027.

The site’s power requirements are so large that the National Grid is building a new substation in Port Talbot to accommodate its needs.

It is poised to take around 70,000 tonnes of scrap metal per week and produce more than three million tonnes of steel a year, making it the biggest electric arc furnace of its kind in the world.

‘Doubling down’ on British steel

Rajesh Nair, the chief executive of Tata Steel UK, said the Government’s tariff changes would ensure Port Talbot is on a “level playing field” with foreign steelmakers.

However, he also warned that the domestic steel industry would thrive only with higher demand from the construction industry.

Mr Nair said: “When there is manufacturing, when there’s growth, steel grows.

“The UK has one of the highest intensities of steel in construction, probably globally. But the reason why it’s still not going is because the construction industry is declining, so you have got to figure out ways of improving demand.

“If you increase construction, you push consumption.”

Asked whether government infrastructure projects such as the Lower Thames Crossing would use at least 50pc British steel, Mr Kyle replied: “We have a commitment to use British supply wherever possible, and wherever practicable, and we’re going to be doubling down on that.

“So of course, wherever there’s opportunity, we’ll be exploring it.”

According to internal assessments for National Highways, which is running the project, 85-90pc of steel for the Lower Thames Crossing could be sourced domestically.

However, there have been warnings that foreign steel could be used instead because it is cheaper.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The NS&I boss who led Britain’s savings bank to chaos
data.description:
When Dax Harkins was appointed head of Britain’s state-owned bank in 2023, hopes were high that he could turn the listing ship around.

For years, National Savings & Investments (NS&I), which looks after around £240bn for 24 million people, had been plagued by customer service meltdowns and a faltering digital revamp.

Executives believed Harkins was the answer: a bright, charming Liverpudlian who had risen through the ranks of the organisation over two decades.

Yet three years on, Harkins has been forced out – his tenure blighted by the same endemic issues his predecessors failed to fix.

Torsten Bell, the pensions minister, announced the bank boss’s resignation to the Commons on Thursday, with a perfunctory nod to Harkins’ “22 years of public service”. He will be replaced by Sir Jim Harra, the former head of HMRC.

Harkins’ departure comes after a Telegraph investigation this week revealed the extent of NS&I’s failings.

The bank stands accused of losing track of investments, delaying payouts and withholding premium bond prizes.

Families of deceased NS&I savers have been forced to call in lawyers to obtain cash that is rightfully theirs, and have incurred thousands in additional costs because of failures at the taxpayer-backed bank dating back years.

It is understood that the savings provider is now seeking a bailout to return misplaced funds to the 37,000 families affected, which could run to £400m. It is expected to be the single biggest payout in the history of the 160-year-old institution.

The bill would come in addition to the £3bn handed to NS&I to assist its transformation rollout, a migration dubbed “Project Rainbow”.

A pay packet of up to £545k

NS&I’s woes date back to the pandemic, when the bank attracted large numbers of savers with market-leading rates as the Government sought to boost its Covid-19 war chest.

But it later slashed deals to just 0.01pc once enough cash had been raised. The dramatic change of tack caused a surge of withdrawals, with savers pulling £12.5bn from their accounts between November 2020 and January 2021.

A deluge of transaction requests, coupled with staff shortages and working from home, proved too much for the institution to handle. Wait times and complaints soared. Call abandonment rates hit 40pc in the last three months of 2020, compared with a normal rate of 5pc.

Ian Ackerley, the bank’s former chief executive, apologised at the time for the poor performance, blaming it on the organisation’s reliance on “paper, people and phones”.

Official rulings on complaints submitted to the Ombudsman show that NS&I has blamed some of its errors on the Covid pandemic, as well as on outsourcing to overseas staff.

A long-standing deal with Atos – a France-based global IT service and consulting firm – means that for years, hundreds of backroom contractors have been based in Chennai, India.

Since 1999, Atos has enjoyed a monopoly on NS&I’s IT, payment and customer service infrastructure, securing a series of contracts worth hundreds of millions of pounds.

In 2020, NS&I launched Project Rainbow, a business transformation programme designed to reduce its running costs and modernise its operations. But the botched tech upgrade exacerbated the bank’s customer service disaster.

The slew of customer service failings did little to impact executive pay. Six NS&I executive board members were paid bonuses ranging between £15,000 and £30,000 in 2022-23.

Speaking to The Telegraph in 2024, Harkins defended the bonuses, saying: “We have worked hard to manage and mitigate those issues.”

Harkins himself was awarded a total pay package of between £545,000 and £550,000 in 2023-24, and £320,000 to £325,000 in 2024-25, according to NS&I’s annual accounts.

His appointment thrust him into the maelstrom of the high-stakes tech revamp, as well as growing scrutiny of NS&I’s deteriorating finances.

Harkins was considered a safe bet: a loyal, earnest NS&I insider who had worked his way up from a humble position in sales, through a string of marketing and product development roles before being appointed to the board in 2014.

As chief executive, he doubled down on the bank’s modernisation drive, insisting that NS&I needed to be “rewired”. However, the costs of the project have soared from an original £1.3bn to £3bn, and the restructuring is now years behind schedule.

The bank has been accused of “throwing good money after bad” by MPs, who cited a £43m spend on consultants, with only vague justifications for why they were needed.

Last month, the public accounts committee (PAC) savaged Harkins’ plan to digitise the bank, calling it a “full-spectrum disaster”. In a damning report, the spending watchdog warned that taxpayers’ money was being pledged to the project without assurance it could even be delivered, saying it presented a risk to both NS&I and its customers.

At a parliamentary hearing in December, Harkins admitted: “A key lesson for us from all of that is that we underestimated the complexity of unpicking 25 years of legacy IT with pretty much a single supplier.”

An NS&I spokesman apologised for the “delays and cost increases” to the transformation programme, but insisted it was “key to deliver the most cost-effective finance for the Government”.

Harkins’ fall from grace is a sobering episode in NS&I’s long history. Launched in 1861, NS&I was first known as the Post Office Savings Bank. It was established to provide a secure place for ordinary citizens to save while funding government spending.

It introduced premium bonds in 1956 and became an independent government department in 1969, rebranding to NS&I in 2002 while maintaining 100pc backing from the Treasury.

The Telegraph’s investigation laid bare the extent of the bank’s ongoing customer service crisis, which shows little sign of abating.

In one case, NS&I was forced to refund a woman’s family for tax interest and legal costs it had incurred after the savings bank lost track of two accounts linked to an investment portfolio.

The family had to pay probate lawyers around £20,000 extra following a three-year investigation to get to the bottom of the missing funds. The error also cost the family around £2,700 in additional inheritance tax interest because the mistake meant they had incorrectly valued the estate for tax purposes.

In another case, NS&I failed to tell the daughter of a deceased saver about “several very large bonds” her mother had owned, and appeared to lose track of another £2,000 in premium bonds the daughter herself owned.

NS&I also referred to her in her dead mother’s name, and on another occasion called her “Mr”. The ombudsman said NS&I only admitted its errors after the daughter persisted, awarding £400.

These failings – multiplied thousands of times over – have proved disastrous. The savings bank is in discussions with the Treasury to repay the tens of thousands of savers whose money is understood to have been misplaced, after failings dating back years.

NS&I now faces a pivotal moment. While Harkins has departed, the chronic failings that precipitated his downfall show no sign of being resolved.

data.title: British factory lines face shutdown within weeks
data.description:
British factories face production cuts within weeks because of surging gas prices, industry bodies have warned.

Ceramics businesses, glass manufacturers and chemical companies could bear the brunt of the global energy crisis because of a heavy reliance on gas, which has soared after attacks on Gulf energy facilities.

These sectors require large amounts of gas to power the high-temperature machines required to manufacture their goods – leaving them more vulnerable to the price spike.

Arjan Geveke, the director of the Energy Intensive Users Group, said: “There’s a high risk that those factories exposed to the spot market prices will cut production in the next few weeks.

“We’ve seen a series of closures, particularly on the chemical side and ceramic side, and that was even before gas prices escalated a few weeks ago.”

Gas prices jumped by 11pc on Thursday after Iran carried out missile attacks on energy sites in Qatar and Saudi Arabia. European benchmark gas prices climbed to €60.72 per megawatt hour – their highest level since January 2023.

Mr Geveke warned that if energy prices remain at their recent highs the Government would need to introduce support within “weeks” or even sooner.

He added that ministers should prepare to intervene “in case this situation prolongs and prices increase further”.

Production cuts in British factories would have wider consequences for many supply chains, triggering knock-on effects across the economy.

The latest escalation in the Iran war adds to fears that companies already grappling with high taxes and levies could be put out of business by soaring energy costs.

Natural gas prices, a key input for UK electricity prices, have jumped by more than 90pc since the war in Iran began.

Frank Aaskov, from UK Steel, said some members had warned they would have to curtail production in the next few weeks.

‘Uncompetitive electricity prices’

He said that while the UK’s “uncompetitive industrial electricity prices have long been the primary barrier to the steel industry’s ability to compete internationally”, the situation was being exacerbated by escalating conflict in the Middle East.

The warnings add to the mounting pressure on Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, to unveil a package of support for companies grappling with a sharp increase in energy bills.

Nadine Bloxsome, the chief executive of the Aluminium Federation, said that unless the Government rolled out financial support measures there was a “real risk that UK manufacturing will become less competitive”.

She said: “UK aluminium manufacturers are already operating under significantly higher energy costs than many of our global competitors, and further increases driven by geopolitical instability only intensify that pressure.”

Any government intervention on energy price for businesses would be expensive. While gas prices are nowhere near the levels seen shortly after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, energy subsidies for businesses cost taxpayers an estimated £18.4bn in 2022.

Tom Jones, from the British Allied Trades Federation, said that the recent energy price spikes are “very destructive” for companies.

He added: “It seems that private industry, particularly manufacturing, takes the brunt of whatever comes along.”

The escalating attacks on energy facilities across the Gulf have led to fears that Britain will face oil and gas shortages in the coming months.

Mr Jones said members were concerned about the “impact of rationing that may come in if we can’t get enough gas or enough oil”.

A Government spokesman said: “We know this is a tough time for our energy intensive industries, who are grappling with increased fossil fuel prices. The best way to tackle this is getting onto home-grown power which we control to bring down bills for good.

“We have published a new steel strategy today that will boost advanced manufacturing across our economy, and we are working with all sectors to understand the impact of the situation in the Middle East and explore potential solutions.”

data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk

data.title: Funeral giant faces investigation over positive cremation reviews
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest funeral directors is facing an investigation into whether staff were asked to write “positive” reviews about its cremation services.

Dignity is being scrutinised by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) after the regulator launched a crackdown on fake and misleading reviews, which will also target Autotrader, Just Eat and Pasta Evangelists.

The watchdog said it will consider whether Dignity told staff to write misleading reviews, giving people “a potentially inaccurate picture of genuine customers’ feedback”.

Dignity operates 46 crematoria across the UK. It provided 70,000 services in 2024, reporting revenues of £326.3m for the year, according to its latest accounts.

The CMA said it would engage with Dignity and gather evidence on whether it had infringed consumer protection laws.

The investigation into Dignity is one of a series of cases announced by the CMA on Friday across sectors, including funerals, food delivery and car sales.

Autotrader and Feefo will be scrutinised over their alleged treatment of negative reviews.

The CMA said it would look into whether a number of one-star reviews, which were moderated by review software business Feefo, were not published on Autotrader’s platform and were not counted towards its overall star ratings.

The regulator said this risked “denying consumers a fully rounded picture of other customers’ experiences”.

An Autotrader spokesman said: “We endeavour always to operate as a responsible and compliant business and will co‑operate fully with the CMA’s investigation.”

A Feefo spokesman added: “We remain entirely confident in our compliance frameworks and look forward to contributing to the CMA’s work in shaping the ‘gold standard’ for digital trust across the automotive sector and beyond.”

Meanwhile, Just Eat faces an investigation into whether its system has inflated certain restaurants’ star ratings, “giving consumers a potentially misleading picture of quality when choosing where to order”.

Pasta Evangelists, the fresh pasta restaurant and delivery group, is also being investigated over whether it offered customers discounts on future orders in exchange for leaving five-star reviews on delivery apps, without disclosing this.

Sarah Cardell, chief executive of the CMA, said: “Fake reviews strike at the heart of consumer trust, with many of us worrying about misleading content when looking at reviews online.

“With household budgets under pressure, people need to know they’re getting genuine information – not reviews or star ratings that have been manipulated to push them towards the wrong choice.

“We’ve given businesses the time to get things right. Now we’re deploying our new powers to tackle some of the most harmful practices head-on.”

A spokesman for Just Eat said: “We are working closely with the Competition and Markets Authority to ensure the reviews and ratings on our platform are clear, transparent and easy to use for all our customers and partners.”

No conclusions have yet been reached on whether any of these companies has broken consumer law.

data.title: Workers killed in fire ‘triggered by explosion’ at car factory
data.description:
Fourteen people have been killed and 59 injured in a fire at a car parts factory in South Korea, suspected to have been caused by an explosion.

Fire crews could not initially enter the factory, in the central city of Daejeon, because of the risk of the building collapsing and sodium stored at the site, which can explode if not properly handled.

More than 500 firefighters, police and emergency personnel were deployed to contain the fire and conduct rescue operations after it broke out at around 1pm local time on Friday.

Searches for the missing workers began late on Friday after officials deployed firefighting robots to cool the structure and conducted a safety inspection.

Around 120 vehicles and equipment including aircraft, an unmanned water cannon vehicle and two robots for hard-to-reach areas, were deployed.

The death toll was originally thought to be 11, but was updated after more bodies were found. Around 170 workers were at the plant when the fire broke out.

Officials have not said what may have caused the fire, which spread rapidly and was extinguished by Saturday afternoon. Witnesses said they had heard an explosion.

Videos and photos from the scene showed thick grey smoke billowing from the complex and some workers jumping from a building. Fire crews could be seen shooting water onto the site from cranes.

Some people were injured when they jumped from the building to escape, while others suffered from smoke inhalation, officials said. As of Saturday morning, 28 people were hospitalised and four of them underwent surgeries for broken bones and other injuries.

“We can confirm the latest death toll has risen to 14, with no one reported missing,” an official from the interior ministry’s department that handles fires and other disasters told AFP.

Police have identified one of the dead workers, while genetic testing is being conducted on others.

Lee Jae Myung, the South Korean president, visited the site and met bereaved families, promising to provide full support for victims and their families.

“The Government will thoroughly investigate the cause of the incident and prepare fundamental measures to prevent such tragedies,” she wrote on X.

Wealthy South Korea has a patchy record on labour safety, with more than 10,000 worksite deaths between 2000 and 2024, according to official statistics.

In September, South Korea sentenced the chief executive of Aricell, a battery maker, to 15 years in prison over one of the country’s worst industrial fires.

The 2024 blaze at the lithium battery factory in Hwaseong, south of the capital Seoul, killed 22 people, most of them Chinese nationals. In its verdict, the Suwon district court said the company had prioritised profit over workers’ safety.
