data.title: Sir David Kirch, reclusive collector of rare banknotes who gave £100 million to the elderly of Jersey
data.description:
​Sir David Kirch, who has died 89, was a London property speculator who assembled what was thought to be Britain’s largest collection of provincial banknotes and the world’s most extensive personal collection of airship memorabilia; but he sold it all in the process of becoming one of the nation’s most generous philanthropists.

In the 1960s the reclusive Kirch had amassed a property empire that at one point included swathes of Kensington and Earl’s Court, as well as the Shaftesbury Theatre in the West End – which he threatened to demolish. Tall and elegant, he was viewed as an enigma. “Often at weekends, he takes his mother down to his cottage at Shoreham-on-Sea, otherwise he sits alone in his 15-room mansion in Hyde Park Gate and watches colour TV after eating his solitary dinner served by his Spanish butler,” reported the Evening Standard. In 1973 he settled as a tax exile in Jersey.

On reaching “three score and ten” in 2006, he decided to mark the milestone by distributing £100 Co-op vouchers at Christmas to every islander aged 70 and over – a commitment he honoured even on discovering that there would be 9,000 recipients. “I am fond of old people because I’m old myself,” he explained. He was delighted to learn that one pensioner had been able to buy a microwave oven, and called it “Mr Kirch”, so that “every time it pinged they thought of me”.

He repeated the scheme in subsequent years – later upping the voucher to £110 – and established a charitable trust to provide homes for pensioners on Jersey. In 2012, on being diagnosed with cancer, he announced that he would leave £100 million, the bulk of his fortune, to the elderly of Jersey.

Observing that his two happiest encounters with money were, first, in the making of it, and second, in the giving away, he proceeded to liquidate his assets to fund his trust. He sold his Rolls-Royce in favour of a Mini, and auctioned his collection of nearly 4,000 English provincial banknotes issued by defunct 19th-century institutions such as Scarborough Old Bank, Glastonbury & Shepton Mallet Bank and many others, their £5 and £10 denominations often fetching four-figure prices. He also sold a more unusual collection of “skit notes” – historic joke banknotes with political or satirical themes, issued by entities such as “the Bank of Love”.

His 15-ton collection of airship relics, sold in 2012, included medals awarded to the crew of the R101 which crashed in France in 1930 and a singed beer bottle salvaged from the wreckage of the Hindenburg in 1937.

David Roderick Kirch was born in Wimbledon on July 23 1936, the son of Leonard Kirch and his wife Margaret. His father had done well as a wholesale meat trader, but chose to keep travelling by train at six o’clock to Smithfield because, if the other traders saw his chauffeured Rolls-Royce, “he’d never get the price he wanted”.

At Tonbridge School, David demonstrated a flair for selling stamps. His commanding officer on National Service agreed that he would make a better businessman than a soldier, and since “I wasn’t clever enough to go to university,” as Kirch recalled, he found a job in the Lloyd’s insurance market.

On the untimely death of his father, aged 59, Kirch inherited £5,000 and launched himself in the meat trade. When his firm was taken over by a Dutch company, who wanted his Kingston cold store rather than his business, he realised that property was a better bet than meat, and bought a stake in a Hampshire estate agency.

He joined forces with his brother Peter in 1962, and they borrowed heavily to buy up chunks of west London, specialising in properties with sitting tenants that could be converted to more lucrative short-term lets. One flat in Courtfield Road was rented to the Rolling Stones; the police stripped off the oak panelling in the drawing room during a drugs bust.

Their first big gamble, as Kirch told an interviewer, was to buy 23 houses in Earl’s Court for more than £250,000 – 90 per cent of it borrowed – though they were producing rental income of only £1,000 a year. “Once you had dealt with sums of a quarter of a million,” he observed, “things do become much easier.”

In the early 1970s the Kirch brothers bought into a quoted company, Peureula (Sumatra) Rubber Estates, which they renamed Peureula Investments and through which they amassed unpayable debts of some £35 million – chiefly to Keyser Ullman, one of the fringe banks heaviest hit by the property crash of 1973. “They can only ever hope to get their money back,” Kirch said in 1976, “which they won’t.”

Having lost nearly everything, David Kirch later rebuilt his portfolio to a value at one point estimated at £250 million.

His enthusiasms as a collector ranged from glass eyebaths to memorabilia from the Crystal Palace (which he dreamed of rebuilding) and a bench that once belonged to Lord Lucan. One particular indulgence was cars; another was gambling. At the Casino de la Fôret at Le Touquet, Ian Fleming challenged him to a race back to their hotel in their sports cars.

Being a covert homosexual, Kirch recalled, was another form of gambling: “Sex was that much more exciting when it was illegal.” He regretted never having children, and observed that his life had spanned an era when his friend, Lord Montagu, could be imprisoned for homosexuality, to the legalisation of gay marriage and adoption by same-sex couples.

In 2013 David Kirch was appointed KBE.

His long-term partner Serge Cuhat died in 2009 in an Alpine ballooning accident.

Sir David Kirch, born July 23 1936, died February 28 2026​

data.title: Savannah Guthrie: I wake up imagining my missing mother’s terror
data.description:
Savannah Guthrie has said she wakes up every night imagining the “terror” her mother must have gone through during her suspected kidnapping.

The US morning news anchor made the comments in her first interview since her mother, Nancy Guthrie, went missing at the end of January.

The presenter struggled to hold back tears as she told her NBC Today co-host Hoda Kotb: “To think of what she went through. I wake up every night in the middle of the night.

“And in the darkness, I imagine her terror. And it is unthinkable, but those thoughts demand to be thought.”

She added that her family was “in agony” over the disappearance of the 84-year-old.

Mrs Guthrie was last seen at 9.45 pm on Jan 31, when her family dropped her off at her home just outside of Tucson, Arizona. She was reported missing the following day.

The case has puzzled investigators, who have turned up few clues about her whereabouts.

Ten days into the search, authorities released a video of a masked, armed figure who appeared to tamper with a security camera at Nancy’s home in the early hours of Feb 1. But law enforcement has not yet been able to identify them.

Drops of Mrs Guthrie’s blood were found on her front porch. Her phone, Apple Watch and vital medications were also still inside the home.

Chris Nanos, the Pima County sheriff who is overseeing the investigation, said DNA from an unknown source had also been found.

Ransom notes sent to media outlets in the days after her disappearance appeared to chart a possible path to Nancy’s safe return.

Savannah Guthrie posted a video of herself and her siblings to her Instagram account and addressed the possible kidnappers directly. They were “ready to talk,” she said. But their calls appear to have gone unanswered.

The Guthrie family has since offered a $1m (£747,000) reward for Nancy’s recovery.

“Someone needs to do the right thing. We are in agony. We are in agony. It is unbearable,” Savannah said in a clip of the interview with Ms Kotb broadcast on Wednesday.

NBC will air more of the interview on Thursday and Friday.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘Rogue builders ran off with £200k and left me homeless’
data.description:
Have you lost money to rogue builders? Email money@telegraph.co.uk

Steve Higgs never envisioned that six years after buying his forever home in Surrey, he would find himself living in a plasterboard shack and £200,000 out of pocket.

But when the building company he hired to redevelop his £625,000 property claimed it had gone bust and abruptly ceased all communication with him, he was left with nothing but foundations and a few half-finished walls despite handing over £170,000 for the work.

Mr Higgs, 39, had set out to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom property in Horley, after he was advised that it had serious asbestos issues.

Since early 2022, he has been forced to live in a makeshift cabin on the grounds of his three-acre estate as his property became uninhabitable.

He last had contact with the building company responsible in January 2025 when they told him they were experiencing “financial troubles” after his home had been demolished.

Mr Higgs, who was paralysed from the chest down in a motorcycle accident when he was 26, said the ordeal had left him emotionally drained, homeless and facing a big hole in his finances.

He said: “It has played havoc on my mental health. They’ve already wasted four years of my life and £170,000. I can’t sit on my hands in this little box any more. I’ve got no windows. It’s just plasterboard walls. I’m literally just living in a cell of my own making.”

It comes as Britain faces a plague of rogue builders that is costing homeowners and the economy billions of pounds a year.

In the past five years alone, homeowners lost an estimated £14.3bn to cowboy builders, while a fear of being ripped off has cost the economy a further £10bn in lost jobs, according to the Federation of Master Builders (FMB) trade body.

Jeremy Gray, of the FMB, said the cost of living crisis had created the “perfect environment” for sham building companies to proliferate.

He said: “There are no barriers to entry so it’s easy to come in and scam people. The rogue builder will come along, undercut a decent builder and then, because people are short of cash, they will go for the cheap option. And it’s only getting worse.”

Mr Higgs paid the building company £113,000 to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom house in Horley, Surrey, after he was advised that issues with asbestos and rotten timber frames made it more cost-effective than his original plan to renovate and extend.

He paid an additional £55,000 for roof truss installation and spent £30,000 on buying materials directly from a roofing supplier.

He said the building company had a strong online presence, including a professional website, Facebook reviews and a range of photos displaying previous work, as well as a branded fleet of vans. Up to four labourers a day would appear on site.

However, when Mr Higgs raised concerns about the quality of bricklaying and incorrect proportions for the new foundations, he was told, in correspondence seen by The Telegraph, that the company had “run into financial troubles”.

Despite numerous calls and emails the company stopped responding to queries about when the remedial work would be carried out.

After a few months, he hired a lawyer to draft a letter before action for £7,000, only to be advised that it could cost up to £120,000 pursuing the claim in a civil court.

He was told that even if he won there was no guarantee he would be able to recoup what he paid out as the company was claiming financial distress.

‘Hopeless’ legal process

Currently all builders carrying out work on private homes are regulated by the Consumer Rights Act 2015. It sets out the standards that consumers can expect when a trader supplies building works, and statutory remedies if those rights are breached.

However, if the company is declared insolvent, options for reclaiming money can be extremely limited and outcomes are typically poor.

Mark Garnier, shadow economic secretary, who has campaigned for more stringent controls on building firms, has previously called the legal process for bringing rogue traders to justice “hopeless”.

If the work was paid for using a credit card, homeowners can claim back up to £30,000 under Section 75. But many rogue traders encourage cash-in-hand payments at a discount to avoid creating a paper trail.

Paula Higgins, at the Homeowners’ Alliance trade body, said “phoenix companies” – whereby the owner of an insolvent business simply rebrands under a different name – was an ongoing problem in the construction sector.

She said: “If you investigate certain directors, they will be setting companies up and closing them down all the time – [the regulation] around it is very weak.”

Despite calls from industry leaders for tighter regulation such as a mandatory national licensing scheme ensuring minimum competency for all builders, the Government has dragged its heels on introducing any new legislation.

Mr Gray said the Government didn’t appear to have any plans to implement controls in the construction sector despite “zero protections” for consumers against rogue builders.

He said: “Anyone can call themselves a builder because they’re not regulated. In certain circumstances you can go through the court, but ultimately if the company folds there’s nothing you can do. You can’t claim anything back. You just have to go to the drawing board.”

To avoid falling prey to rogue builders, Ms Higgins said the most important steps were to get a contract in place, avoid paying for projects upfront and in cash, and to carry out extensive research into the company beyond its own website and social media channels.

She also warned against any company that is available at short notice. “The best builders are the ones you have to wait for,” she said, adding that everyone should look on Companies House to see for how long a business has been operating.

For word of mouth recommendations, ensure their previous work is similar to what you are asking for and check previous projects yourself, she added.

Mr Higgs said he had been quoted £23,000 for remedial work, including widening the existing foundation to the correct size. The rest of the project is expected to cost another £90,000, plus VAT.

“It’s pretty much looking at the same figures again,” he said.

Mr Higgs receives an income from a personal injury trust, but said the financial impact would be long-lasting. “It’s definitely thrown a spanner in the works,” he said. “It’s turned what was meant to be a five-year build plan into a 10-year plan.”

A spokesman for the Department for Business and Trade said: “Rogue builders are a scourge for hard-working families. Anyone who is affected can contact their local authority who have new powers to hand out tougher fines against businesses who rip off consumers.”

data.title: £50bn construction giant abandons London stock market
data.description:
Building materials giant CRH is to quit the London Stock Exchange (LSE) in a further blow to Britain’s standing as a global financial hub.

CRH, which is worth nearly £54bn, said it had decided to exit the UK market because it was hamstrung by high regulatory costs and low levels of trading activity.

The firm’s shares will now be traded only in New York, where they had been listed in addition to London since September 2023.

The move serves as yet another setback for London’s struggling stock market, with CRH joining a string of high-profile companies that have defected to New York in search of higher valuations.

Online payments company Wise, worth £12bn, last year transferred its main share listing to the US, saying the move would allow better access to the world’s most liquid stock market.

Meanwhile, AstraZeneca said in September that it would upgrade its presence on the New York exchange in what bosses described as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

While the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain based in the UK, the announcement has stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker and most valuable business could ultimately use the move as a stepping stone to quitting the LSE.

CRH had already declared New York as its primary listing and had been removed from the FTSE 100 index.

The company, which is based in Dublin, said a review launched last month had concluded that quitting London completely was “in the best interests of CRH and its shareholders”.

It said: “CRH carefully considered, among other factors, the level of trading activity for its ordinary shares on the LSE as well as the additional cost, regulatory and administrative obligations arising from retaining the LSE listings.”

The firm said it had asked the Financial Conduct Authority to cancel its London listing, with the move expected to be completed by Apr 20.

CRH said on switching to the dual listing three years ago that the decision had also been partly driven by a US push for onshoring manufacturing activity, something that has gathered pace under Donald Trump.

CRH, which was founded in 1970 via a merger of two Irish cement and quarrying companies, already counts North America as its biggest market.

data.title: Dyson suffers £440m sales hit from ‘damaging’ Trump tariffs
data.description:
Dyson has suffered a £440m hit to sales after falling foul of Donald Trump’s tariffs.

The electronics company, founded by Sir James Dyson, said revenues slipped from £6.57bn to £6.13bn last year, which it also blamed on weak consumer confidence and changes in the pound.

Despite the drop in sales, profits were up after a round of job cuts reduced the company’s costs.

In its latest results, released on Thursday, Dyson said it had launched a record number of products last year and invested £400m in research and development.

It is the second year in a row that Dyson’s sales have fallen, after 22 consecutive years of non-stop revenue growth.

The company said it was affected by low consumer confidence in the US, Germany and China, although it said sales grew in Britain.

President Trump imposed tariffs of up to 24pc on US imports from Malaysia, where Dyson manufactures many of its products, although this was later cut to 19pc. It also manufactures in the Philippines, which was also hit by a 19pc tariff before they were voided by the Supreme Court last month.

The company raised prices in the US last year, citing “global economic conditions”.

Following the results, Hanno Kirner, Dyson’s chief executive, said: “In 2025, the US tariffs were particularly damaging. However, Dyson fared well, with earnings and operating profit up significantly and costs markedly down.”

Operating profits increased from £520m to £600m, while earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation, another measure of profitability, rose from £940m to £1.1bn.

Sir James said: “We operate in highly competitive markets and Dyson is relentlessly focused on inventing the best-performing products.”

Dyson, best known for its cordless vacuum cleaners, has expanded into haircare products, air purifiers and robot cleaners in recent years as it faced increasing competition from cut-price rivals.

It has also sought to differentiate itself using artificial intelligence, including a new robot vacuum and scrubber that can identify and remove stains.

Mr Kirner said the company planned to release a record number of products “at a range of different price points”, which he said would allow it to reach more consumers.

Dyson, which now has its headquarters in Singapore, cut around 1,000 roles in Britain in mid-2024, roughly a third of its workforce in the country.

The company, founded in Wiltshire in 1991, is fully owned by the Dyson family.

Sir James, one of Britain’s richest men with an estimated net worth of £20.8bn, recently bought 50pc of rugby union club Bath.

data.title: Seven common home improvements that won’t pay off when you sell
data.description:
If you’re considering selling your home this year, you might be tempted to fix everything you can before putting it on the market.

Whether it’s replacing your outdated kitchen or getting new carpets, many sellers think a bit of investment will help secure a higher price or a quicker sale. But some fixes simply won’t pay off.

Certain improvements will waste both your time and money, and may well get replaced by the new owner as soon as they move in.

Angela Kerr, from the HomeOwners Alliance, said: “Home buyers realise a home has been loved and lived in, so will have some wear and tear. While in an ideal world, your property would look perfect, you need to prioritise spending money on essential repairs and minor redecoration that will help your home sell faster.”

To maximise value and minimise stress, knowing where to hold back on improvements may be just as useful as knowing where to take action.

Here, Telegraph Money has asked the experts which common property fixes sellers shouldn’t bother spending money on.

1. A kitchen replacement

Think twice before going all out and installing a shiny new kitchen, especially if your current one is functional.

There’s a risk you could spend thousands on this outlay shortly before selling, only to discover it does little to increase your home’s value.

Tom Evans, sales director at estate agency Purplebricks, said: “Most buyers prefer to personalise this area for themselves. As long as it works – and isn’t too shabby or flimsy – I would advise against the time and expense of upgrading.”

If you’re looking for a quick fix in the kitchen that won’t break the budget, consider replacing just the cabinet doors, handles or worktops to give your existing room a bit of a revamp.

2. Adding an extension

Another common mistake sellers make before putting their property on the market is going all out on a loft conversion or extension.

Marc von Grundherr, director of estate agency Benham and Reeves, said: “What sellers should avoid is embarking on expensive projects purely to try to increase the sale price in the short term. Major extensions, loft conversions or structural layout changes rarely make financial sense if the only goal is to sell the property immediately afterwards.”

While projects like this can add value, they can also be very pricey – not to mention time-consuming.

Mr Evans said: “An expensive conversion is unlikely to ever correspond with a particularly elevated property price. There is a risk that if you, say, spend £50,000 on the extension, it might only add £40,000 to the property.”

Another big no-no is “over-improving” the area.

Mr Evans added: “If your home is already at the top price range for your street, there is little point in putting more time and money into the property pre-sale.”

3. Redesigning the garden

If you’re considering giving your outside area an overhaul, think again.

Mr Evans said: “Another area people tend to really push the boat out on is garden design. There’s a dizzying array of things you could do to your garden, and an eye-watering sum you could plough into making it look great. But from experience, this rarely makes a difference to the asking price – or the speed or amount the property sells for.”

Of course, you shouldn’t neglect your garden, either. But rather than spending money on a redesign that isn’t worth the time or money, carrying out smaller, more manageable tasks, such as tidying lawns, weeding flower beds and repairing fences.

It’s all about “kerb appeal”, according to Dominick Brown, director of buying agency Prime Purchase.

He said: “Think about that all-important first impression or kerb appeal. Is the driveway clear? Does it need additional gravel to fill in bumps? Does the house present as well as it possibly can at first glance? See if there are any slipped tiles which need to be corrected, bins hidden, and flower beds planted or tidied.”

4. New white goods

While you might like the idea of potential buyers setting eyes on a top-of-the-range fridge, freezer or dishwasher when they come to view your home, buying these appliances could be a waste of money.

Ms Kerr said: “You’re unlikely to recoup what you’ve spent when you sell. If you need to replace an appliance because it’s broken, consider a second-hand [one] to keep costs down.”

5. A bathroom upgrade

As with your kitchen, there’s a risk that if you pour lots of cash into bathroom renovations, it could be money down the drain.

Mr Evans said: “There’s no point replacing a perfectly usable bathroom suite with an expensive one. If you do, there’s every chance the potential buyer will have different views on what their dream bathroom might be.”

As long as the taps and fixtures are working properly – and you don’t have any leaks – you are better off focusing your efforts on tasks such as deep cleaning, regrouting tiles and replacing worn flooring.

6. Expensive home improvements

While it’s not a bad idea to give your home a fresh lick of paint if needed, be sparing, as doing too much could end up putting buyers off.

Your choice of decor is important, too. Give bold colours a wide berth, and focus instead on using just a fresh coat of neutral paint to make your home feel lighter and brighter.

Mr Evans said: “Any home improvement that involves personal taste – or which is a trend – is not worth adjusting. If the imperfections are just cosmetic, and something that the buyer can live with, we would recommend leaving well alone.”

7. New carpets

If your existing carpets feel old and worn, you may be dying to refresh your home with brand new ones. But once again, this is not a sensible use of your cash.

Ms Kerr said: “Instead, for a much cheaper fix, get your carpets deep cleaned. You’ll be amazed at the difference it makes.”

You can either pay a professional to do the job for you. Or, to keep the costs down further still, you could hire the equipment and do the job yourself.

What is worth spending money on?

When preparing a home for sale, it’s important to spend time and effort on the things that genuinely reassure a buyer, rather than shelling out on upgrades that they may not like or will not be reflected in the sale price.

Mr von Grundherr said: “The priority should always be the fundamentals of the property. Issues such as damp, visible structural problems, electrical faults, heating issues or missing roof tiles are all immediate red flags because they raise concerns about the wider condition of the home.

“Even relatively minor problems, such as cracked plaster, can make house-hunters question whether there are deeper issues beneath the surface.”

Addressing these types of repairs before listing can make a big difference to buyer confidence.

Amy Reynolds, from estate agency Antony Roberts, said: “The key is to focus on issues that could derail a sale – rather than chasing perfection. If the rest of the property is in good condition, it is usually worth honing in on issues that regularly trigger down-valuations, renegotiations or even collapsed sales.”

This includes any problems that may get flagged up in a survey.

Mr Brown said: “For example, any lingering damp is likely to raise concerns for both buyers and their surveyors. Failing to remedy it will almost certainly become an issue during price negotiations or following a survey.”

Roof repairs are another no-brainer; you need to get these issues fixed as they will be visible to a buyer before they even step in the door, whether they get a building survey or not.

Remember the small ‘fixes’

If you are trying to move quickly, there are plenty of smaller tasks you can do yourself, which can improve the appeal of your home – without significant expense. Decluttering and deep cleaning are a great starting point.

Ms Reynolds said: “Focus on presentation – fresh paint, good lighting and declutter.”

Get rid of limescale, clean and repair tile grout, and wax wooden floors.

It’s not all about what a property looks like; make sure it smells good too. Ms Kerr said: “Try different products to get your bathroom to shine, get rid of odours, hang up fresh towels. This will make the place more appealing and allow potential buyers to imagine living there.”

Mr Evans added: “Whether it’s touching up scuffed paintwork, filling in small cracks in the plastering, replacing broken handles or light fittings, re-sealing bathrooms and kitchens, or deep cleaning carpets – a neutral, clean and well-maintained property will sell better than a stylish but tired one.”

If you’re not sure which jobs to prioritise before putting your home on the market, speak to an estate agent about which fixes are likely to make your home more likely to sell.

Ms Kerr said: “If you want to know if anything in your home is a potential deal breaker, then ask a good local estate agent. They will know what prospective buyers in your area are looking for.”

data.title: Best cash Isas: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A cash Isa is similar to a savings account, but with a crucial difference – you don’t pay tax on the interest you earn.

It is one of four types of adult individual savings accounts (Isas) – the others are stocks and shares Isas, innovative finance Isas and lifetime Isas.

You can currently save up to £20,000 per tax year, which is known as the Isa allowance. In the 2025 Budget, Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced plans to reduce this to £12,000 from April 2027 for savers under the age of 65; younger savers will have to use an investment Isa if they wish to use the remaining £8,000 of their allowance.

You can either deposit this sum into one account or spread it across several, and you can transfer your Isa savings between providers if you want to take advantage of a better rate elsewhere.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains how cash Isas work, covering:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best cash Isas in March
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa
  • Why choose a cash Isa?
  • Cash Isa FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables below show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best cash Isas in March 2026

Find the best cash Isa for you using the tables below.

Best easy-access cash Isas

These accounts usually allow you to make as many deposits and withdrawals as you like – but this flexibility can come with lower rates. For the savings equivalent, you can see our guide to the best easy-access savings accounts.

Best variable rate cash Isas

Accounts in the table below show the best Isa rates on the market for accounts that offer a variable rate of interest. While this could increase at any time – which can be valuable when rates are on an upward trend – it could also decrease.

Best notice cash Isas

These accounts require you to give a certain amount of notice to your provider when you want to make a withdrawal, indicating the number of days you must wait until your money is released. You can usually make as many withdrawals as you like – but be sure to check the account terms first.

If this way of saving suits you, make sure you check our guide to the best notice accounts for alternative options.

Best fixed-rate cash Isas

These Isas require you to lock your money away for a specified period of time. The rate of interest is guaranteed throughout this period, which can give you peace of mind should other rates take a tumble.

For the savings account equivalent, which you might want to use if you’ve used up your Isa allowance, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For other types of tax-free saving, see our guide to the best Junior cash Isas for under-18s. Or, if you’re saving to buy a home or fund your retirement, don’t miss our guide to the best lifetime Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa

Choosing the best cash Isa for you can be tricky, as it depends on why you’re saving and how you want to save – but there should be options to suit everyone.

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare, said: “For savers that are more tax savvy, cash Isas may be the ideal option as you receive a £20,000 Isa allowance each year and can pay into multiple Isas within the same period providing this limit is not exceeded. Isas aren’t too dissimilar to their traditional savings counterparts with easy access, notice, fixed and regular accounts being an option.

“It is worth considering that for the variable rate options, interest could increase or decrease over time, and those Isas that are fixed for an agreed term will either not allow earlier access or may deduct a certain amount of interest.”

Cash Isas can also be an effective way of saving for the longer term.

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Cash Isas have certainly seen a resurgence of late. Looking at what may be coming in Labour’s Budget, and the rhetoric around higher taxes, cash Isas should be the first port of call for all savers at the moment.

“If you’ve got £20,000 or less, definitely go for a cash Isa first and ensure your money is protected from tax. If you’re fortunate enough to have more than £20,000 to save in an Isa, then you can look at other saving accounts as well.”

Why choose a cash Isa?

Cash Isas are beneficial for many savers as they offer an easy way to make sure your savings interest is sheltered from income tax.

Their popularity took a dip when the personal savings allowance (PSA) was introduced in 2016, as it means some savers can earn up to £1,000 in tax-free savings interest each tax year. When interest rates are low, only those with large savings pots or high incomes come into the scope of paying tax on savings interest.

However, savings rates have been high for some time; until recently it was not uncommon to be earning 5pc. Someone with a £1,000 PSA could face a savings tax bill with £20,000 in savings – a problem that doesn’t occur with a cash Isa. What’s more, your growth is protected as it compounds for years into the future, which has helped some savers become Isa millionaires.

The main issue that comes with a cash Isa is the £20,000 Isa limit on how much you can pay into it each tax year. If you want to move a large savings deposit in order to protect your returns from tax, it may need to be moved gradually over several tax years.

In some cases, interest rates offered by cash Isas can be lower than their savings equivalent. Providers are well aware of the extra tax benefit Isas offer, and know they don’t have to be as competitive with rates.

It’s important to shop around and find the most competitive rate you can. Cash Isa rates can be lower than their savings account counterparts, but the tax-free benefits of an Isa will often outweigh the loss of a little interest.

If you’re not sure whether you could be in danger of having to pay tax on your savings interest, try the Telegraph Money savings tax calculator – simply enter your savings, interest rate and tax band and it can tell you whether you’d be better off getting an Isa.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no limit to the number of cash Isas you can have – but it’s a good idea not to have so many that you lose track of them.

You can hold cash Isas you’ve paid into in past tax years, and – now that Isa rules have changed – you can also open and pay into as many as you like in the same tax year. The key thing is to make sure you don’t pay in more than £20,000 across all Isas in the same tax year.

Yes, you can combine Isas into one account – but in order to do this without affecting the tax status of the money, or your Isa allowance, you must move the cash via an Isa transfer.

There are quite a few rules to bear in mind when you do this – you can find out more in our comprehensive guide to Isa transfers.

This depends on the rate you’re earning and the rate of inflation. At the time of writing, lots of cash Isas can beat inflation, but you might need to shop around for them. It’s a good idea to keep an eye on what’s happening with inflation versus the best Isa rates so you can gauge whether or not you have a competitive deal.

Our inflation calculator shows the effect rising prices can have on your savings.

It’s never too late to put money in an Isa to benefit from its tax-free status. However, if you’re planning to deposit money very close to the end of the tax year on April 5, there’s a chance you could miss the deadline.

If this happens, you can still save the money – it will just be taken from the following year’s allowance instead.

The annual Isa allowance resets at the start of a tax year on April 6. You can choose to put this entire amount into one account, or split it among several.

Mr Hicks said: “Isas were typically seasonal, with a big spike in demand around the end of the tax year. They are much less timely now and every year the cash Isa season gets longer and longer.”

data.title: NS&I boss sacked amid cover-up claims
data.description:
The boss of National Savings and Investments has been forced out of his job amid fears of a cover-up at the state-backed bank.

Dax Harkins resigned as the chief executive of NS&I on Thursday, just two days after The Telegraph revealed that £476m belonging to 37,500 dead savers had been withheld from their families in a scandal dating back to 2008.

NS&I customers have been complaining to the bank, as well as the financial ombudsman, for years, while several parliamentary reports have warned of systemic failings at the bank.

The Treasury claimed it had only learnt of the missing millions in December. However, it forced Mr Harkins out only after The Telegraph exposed the scandal this week.

However, The Telegraph understands that the Treasury blocked Mr Harkins’s bonus early last year.

In an urgent statement to the Commons on Thursday, Torsten Bell, the pensions minister, said that “warning signs” had been missed repeatedly.

Addressing MPs, he said: “What is now clear is that NS&I did not respond to those warning signs as fully as I, and more importantly, their customers, would expect. Nor did the last government act.”

Mr Bell compared the crisis to “serious failings” by Santander in 2018 over processing the accounts and investments of deceased customers.

He said there was “widespread coverage” of the scandal at the time, at which point people “within NS&I ... realised that this could pose problems for them”.

Critics have demanded answers over “how this was allowed to happen and why it took so long to act”.

The Treasury denied Mr Harkins his annual performance-related bonus last year, with officials insisting this was because of his failure to deliver a flagship digital modernisation programme.

Atos, the firm at the heart of the project, was also responsible for managing NS&I’s bereavement services until last year, when it was relieved of its duties. NS&I had already taken customer service responsibilities away from the firm following poor performance.

The bank also declined to join the official Tell Us Once service, which allows families to send one message to notify all government departments of a bereavement and was first launched in 2011.

It subsequently failed to sign up to the equivalent for banks and building societies, known as the death notification service, which launched in 2018, despite calls from consumer rights campaigners.

Sir Mel Stride, the shadow chancellor, said: “This is a shocking failure at the heart of a state-backed bank, with tens of thousands of savers denied money that is rightfully theirs.

“Heads have rightly rolled – but serious questions remain about how this was allowed to happen and why it took so long to act.

“The Government must ensure every penny is repaid swiftly and guarantee taxpayers are not left footing the bill for yet another public sector fiasco.”

Robert Jenrick, Reform UK’s Treasury spokesman, said: “This scandal is incompetence on a staggering scale. Throwing Dax Harkins under the bus does not mean that Rachel Reeves and Torsten Bell no longer have serious questions to answer.”

Mr Bell confirmed that there would be a compensation scheme for the families of customers affected, though questions remain over how it will be funded.

It is understood that either NS&I or the Treasury will pay, meaning taxpayers may still be on the hook.

Recipients also face being taxed on any compensation they receive, as well as on any money returned to them. This is because the sums could alter the value of deceased savers’ estates for tax purposes, meaning they could breach the inheritance tax threshold.

Mr Bell said: “We also recognise there may be tax implications for affected estates and want to avoid bereaved families facing disproportionate disruption and administrative costs as a result of this error.

“We are exploring what support can be provided and will set this out alongside [the] NS&I delivery plan in May.”

Mr Bell added that taxpayers would not be forced to fund a bailout, saying the missing money was a “tracing issue”.

Sir Jim Harra, a former head of HM Revenue and Customs, has taken charge of NS&I with immediate effect, and will lead a massive effort to reunite the families of dead savers with the money are owed.

Mr Bell has ordered him to publish a report explaining how this will be done by May, detailing exactly how many customers are affected, how their savings will be returned plus interest, and what additional compensation will be paid.

Sir Jim’s appointment has raised eyebrows after he presided over a string of customer service failings at the tax office.

Andrew Griffith, the shadow business secretary, said the saga “raises questions about the Treasury’s own supervision, including what they have done since being informed on Dec 18 2025 and The Telegraph breaking this story this week”.

He added: “There must be no rewards for failure, so ministers should be using ‘claw back’ to recoup past payments to the CEO and management.

“At its heart, this is an epic failure to deliver what should be a simple suite of savings products which many private sector providers would have delivered in months at a fraction of the price.”

Treasury hand-picked new man on NS&I board

Former NS&I officials have gone on to high office. They include Lord Lemos, who was chairman of the NS&I board until last year, when he quit to take up an appointment in the Whip’s Office in the House of Lords.

The Treasury replaced him with Luke Jensen, a seasoned retail executive who was hand-picked as an “exceptional appointment without competition”.

Critics have raised concerns over the qualifications of senior NS&I officials.

Unlike high street banks, NS&I does not legally need to follow strict regulations on how it operates that are set by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), although it is still expected to meet the same standards.

FCA rules state that the management of banks must be staffed by people with “adequate skills and experience [who] act with integrity”. It does not require that banking executives hold certain qualifications.

Neil Record, a former Bank of England economist, said: “In commercial banking, there used to be banking exams and qualifications, which if you wanted to rise up from the bottom you would need to take those exams. Modern banking and particularly investment banking is a very different animal and you do not take exams.”

Mr Harkins was appointed chief executive of NS&I in April 2023, having worked his way up the organisation from the sales department after graduating from university with a degree in management and chemical sciences.

He took home £300,000 in salary and pension perks last year.

NS&I, launched in 1861, was originally known as the Post Office Savings Bank. It was established to provide a secure place for ordinary citizens to save while funding government spending.

It introduced Premium Bonds in 1956 and became an independent government department in 1969, rebranding to NS&I in 2002.

The bank, which looks after £240bn for some 24 million customers, has apologised for the failings.

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ordinary taxpayers will pay for Labour’s energy price support
data.description:
Panicked talk of a taxpayer-funded bailout for UK households to counter the prospect of spiralling fuel bills is further unnerving bond markets and needs to be nipped in the bud. Even if the Government wanted to, markets wouldn’t allow it.

Ministers will be hoping it all blows over by the time the next price cap ends in July, which is why the idea of support was studiously avoided at Monday’s emergency meeting of senior Cabinet members. They don’t want to further frighten the horses.

Like Banquo’s ghost, the notion nevertheless hovered over the proceedings. Pressure for a catch-all bailout is mounting fast. The targeted support, already under consideration as a backup, looks like an equally hard sell. Even if morally a good idea, it suffers from three self-evident drawbacks.

One is that it will anger all those not included, particularly households close to the cut-off point.

A second related complication is precisely where to draw the line. It’s not just those on Universal Credit who have difficulty paying fuel bills; it also depends crucially on what type of dwelling you live in and where. Any such scheme would be riddled with unfairness.

The other problem with targeted support is that, in macroeconomic terms, its impact is never going to be anything more than marginal, and would therefore amount to just more welfare in a country that can no longer afford it.

To act as a meaningful fiscal counter to the recessionary effects of rising energy prices, support would have to be universal. But that’s pretty much out of the question after the damage done to the public finances by Liz Truss’s Energy Price Guarantee Scheme, and might in any case be counterproductive.

One look at bond markets tells you that the UK and much of the rest of Europe can no longer indulge in fiscal largesse of this sort, even if thought otherwise desirable.

The yield on the 10-year gilt surged through the 5pc barrier on Monday, and on the two-year gilt through 4.7pc – that’s higher than gilt yields got during Truss’s mini-Budget fiasco three and a half years ago.

These pressures eased back a little after Trump did his customary “Grand Old Duke of York” trick. Having marched us up to the top of the hill, he’s marched us down again. But who knows what he’ll be saying on Truth Social tomorrow.

In any case, the bond vigilantes are back in force; any broadly based fiscal countermeasures will be severely punished by higher interest rates.

The scale of the consequent monetary squeeze would therefore very likely more than cancel out the fiscal expansionism.

Some are partially blaming poor old Andrew Bailey, the Governor of the Bank of England, for another foot-in-mouth blunder when he announced last week that there would be no change in the Bank Rate.

His remarks, in combination with published minutes from the preceding Bank of England Monetary Policy Committee meeting, were widely interpreted as particularly and unnecessarily hawkish, thereby contributing to the rise in market interest rates.

So much for the Ben Bernanke review, which was meant to improve the Bank’s analysis and messaging. Instead of a settled view when minutes are published, we now get each of the nine members of the committee individually sounding off. There’s no clarity at all.

The increasingly unstable structure of ownership in today’s gilt market makes matters worse still.

The once-big source of gilt buying – defined benefit pension schemes – has largely gone, with liability-matching strategies now complete.

Into their place have stepped overseas and hedge fund investors, both of them capricious and short-term in their approach.

Trading strategies are, moreover, often driven by computer algorithms rather than human beings. Whenever the Bank of England says anything, it is instantly analysed by AI, which then classifies it as either hawkish or doveish relative to what went before. Herd-like behaviour in markets is further exaggerated.

It is all too possible that today’s volatility will end up in forced selling, in which case the Bank of England may again find itself having to perform the same service as during the Truss mini-Budget debacle, when it became a buyer of last resort.

Maybe Bailey got his messaging wrong, but it didn’t seem to me that he was saying anything more than the obvious – that the world has changed, and Bank Rate is in a holding pattern until we get a clearer understanding of where we are heading.

His problem is that steeply rising energy prices threaten to be both inflationary and recessionary at the same time.

After Bailey spoke, things initially darkened further, then improved a bit when Trump appeared to seek peace. But it hasn’t made the central dilemma for monetary policy any easier.

The fact is that markets are punishing the UK more than others. That’s possibly because inflation has historically been a bigger problem in Britain than elsewhere and continues to look stickier than in much of the rest of Europe.

Markets are pricing in at least three rate rises by the end of the year for the UK, rather than the two cuts that were expected before the war – an effective tightening of more than one percentage point.

This, in turn, would virtually destroy the fiscal headroom the Chancellor announced in her Spring Statement by further increasing the Government’s debt-servicing costs, raising the possibility of more tax rises or spending cuts into the teeth of a contracting economy to patch things up.

The three European countries most affected by Monday’s bond market sell-off were the UK, Italy and Greece, all of which are notably more dependent on gas for their energy needs than much of the rest of Europe. The calculation is that high gas prices are likely to prove more persistent because of damaged liquefied natural gas infrastructure in the Gulf than higher oil prices, increasing the pressure for bailouts in gas-dependent countries.

At any rate, Britain seems to have a particular problem with fiscal credibility. The prospect of a Left-leaning challenge to Sir Keir Starmer in a few months’ time has made matters worse still.

When prices rise because of reduced supply, the effect is to squeeze demand until the two come back into balance. It is the market’s way of rationing consumption.

It could, of course, be done by means other than the price mechanism. For instance, during the three-day week of 1974, the Government issued petrol coupons, imposed speed limits and enacted a rolling series of blackouts to reduce energy use.

Measures like these might seem socially more equitable, but they don’t alter the underlying reality, which is that supply has become a whole lot more constrained.

Sorry to say, but people are just going to have to learn to suck it up and cut their consumption accordingly. Spending initiatives that encourage households to maintain their consumption by heating their homes to feel like a Caribbean beach, turning on the air-conditioning, or driving rather than walking are the opposite of what’s required and will eventually only further pole-axe the public finances.

This article is an extract from The Telegraph’s Economic Intelligence newsletter. Sign up here to get exclusive insight from two of the UK’s leading economic commentators – Ambrose Evans-Pritchard and Jeremy Warner – delivered direct to your inbox every Tuesday

data.title: Co-op threatens to sack staff who tackle shoplifters
data.description:
Co-op store staff have been warned that they face disciplinary action if they intervene to prevent shoplifters from stealing goods.

Bosses at the supermarket chain have told staff not to challenge shoplifters because of the risk that it could lead to violent confrontations with thieves.

A spokesman for the retailer said: “Safety is always our first priority, as too often retail crime can be a trigger for violence and abuse, so we ask our store colleagues not to put themselves in harm’s way, with the sole aim of protecting them.”

The move has provoked a backlash from staff amid record rates of shoplifting last year, with nearly three store thefts a minute being reported to police.

One worker told The Sun: “It’s ridiculous – we have been told not to stop them walking out with stolen gear and that laying a finger on them is a sackable offence.

“They come in knowing they can fill their bags and walk out without paying. They took so much wine that we locked it in a cabinet but everything else in the store is up for grabs. We have to stand and watch.”

Kemi Badenoch said last week that the public should intervene if they saw thieves stealing from shops.

Asked whether she would step in, the Tory leader said: “It depends on how big they are. But yes, broadly, it would be my instinct. I think people should look after their personal safety. I think those people who are stronger should do more.”

Shoplifting has soared since a 2014 law change meant anybody stealing less than £200 of ­products would not be jailed. Labour has ditched the limit as part of a crackdown on retail crime.

It hit a record high of 530,643 offences reported to police in the year to March 2025, a 20 per cent increase on the previous year’s total of 444,022.

But it has levelled off since police chiefs struck an agreement with the Government under which they pledged to attend shoplifting cases if there was violence against a store worker, a suspected thief was detained, or officers were needed to secure evidence.

A Co-op spokesperson said: “We have been the leading voice in campaigning for stronger protection for shopworkers which resulted in a new law to make abusing a shopworker an offence. 

“Retail crime fell at Co-op last year, following our continued investment in preventative security measures and partnerships with local police forces, plus increased police attendance and Government action.”

The Co-op has invested tens of millions of pounds in improving store safety and security, which had included the tactical deployment of specially trained guards able to detain shoplifters until police arrive.

The supermarket added that it had also introduced the latest interactive CCTV, body-worn cameras that capture real-time audio and visual footage at the touch of a button, fortified kiosks, the use of dummy or empty packaging to deter bulk theft and deployment of undercover security guards to watch for attempted store thefts.

Home Office figures suggest that police are bringing more shoplifters to justice. The proportion of shoplifting offences resulting in a charge has increased from 17.9 per cent last year to 19.2 per cent in the year to March 2025.

However, more than 800 shoplifting offences a day are still going unsolved and are closed without a suspect being identified, according to Home Office statistics.
data.author: The telegraph
